1:1.text: Amos. His name is written in a different manner, in Hebrew, from that of the third among the minor prophets, (Worthington) though St. Augustine has confounded them.
Ezechias. He wrote this title towards the end of his life, or it was added by Esdras, etc.

1:2.text: Earth. He apostrophises these insensible things, (Calmet) because they contain all others, and are the most durable.

1:5.text: Sad. This was spoken after Ozias had given way to pride, when the Ammonites, etc., began to disturb Juda, (4 Kings 15:37., and 2 Paralipomenon 27:7.) under Joathan, who was a good prince, but young.
Enemies. At the last siege, (St. Jerome) or rather when Jerusalem was taken by the Chaldeans.
Many, from the highest to the lowest, had prevaricated: but God always preserved his Church.

1:6.text: Sad. This was spoken after Ozias had given way to pride, when the Ammonites, etc., began to disturb Juda, (4 Kings 15:37., and 2 Paralipomenon 27:7.) under Joathan, who was a good prince, but young.
Enemies. At the last siege, (St. Jerome) or rather when Jerusalem was taken by the Chaldeans.
Many, from the highest to the lowest, had prevaricated: but God always preserved his Church.

1:7.text: Sad. This was spoken after Ozias had given way to pride, when the Ammonites, etc., began to disturb Juda, (4 Kings 15:37., and 2 Paralipomenon 27:7.) under Joathan, who was a good prince, but young.
Enemies. At the last siege, (St. Jerome) or rather when Jerusalem was taken by the Chaldeans.
Many, from the highest to the lowest, had prevaricated: but God always preserved his Church.

1:8.text: Cucumbers. Or melons, which grew in the fields, and huts were erected for guards, till they were gathered.

1:10.text: Sodom. Juda is so styled reproachfully, (Calmet) because the princes imitated the crimes of that devoted city, Ezechiel 16:49., and infra[Isaias] Isaias 2:6., and 3:9.

1:11.src: St. Jerome; Psalm 49:9., Amos 5:21., and Jeremias 6:20.; Theodoret
1:11.text: Victims. Without piety, they are useless. God tolerated bloody victims to withdraw the people from idolatry, but he often shewed that they were not of much importance, in order that they might be brought to offer the sacrifice of the new law, which eminently includes all the rest.

1:14.text: Bearing. Hebrew, etc., "pardoning," (Calmet) or "bearing." Septuagint, "I will no longer pardon your sins."

1:16.text: He seems to allude to baptism.

1:18.text: Accuse me. If I punish you without cause.

1:22.text: Water. There is no sincerity in commerce.
Teachers give false interpretations of the law.
Iniquity abounded before the destruction of Jerusalem by the Chaldeans and Romans.

1:24.text: Comfort. I will take complete vengeance under Joathan, (4 Kings 15:37.) Achaz, etc.

1:25.text: Tin. I will reform abuses in the reign of Ezechias, but much more by establishing the Church of Christ, which shall be the faithful city.

1:26.text: Judges. The Jews explain this of the judges, and priests, who governed after the captivity; though it refer rather to the apostles, etc.

1:29.text: Idols. Protestants, "oaks, which ye have desired, and ye shall be confounded for the gardens," etc.
The groves were sacred to Venus, and the gardens to Adonis, and were scenes of the greatest immorality and profanation, Isaias 65:3.

1:31.text: It. The efforts of Achan and Ezechias against the enemy proved in vain.

2:1.text: Jerusalem. Many particular prophecies are blended with the general one, which regards Christ.

2:2.text: Days. The whole time of the new law, from the coming of Christ till the end of the world, is called in the Scripture the last days; because no other age, or time shall come after it, but only eternity.
It is therefore styled the last hour, 1 John 2:
Mountains. This shews the perpetual visibility of the Church of Christ: for a mountain upon the top of mountains cannot be hid.
This evidently regards the Church, Matthew 5.
The Jews can never shew the fulfillment of this prophecy in any material temple. Micheas 4:1. copies this text.

2:3.text: Jerusalem. Our Saviour preached there, and in some sense the religion established by him, may be esteemed a reform, or accomplishment of the old law.

2:4.text: War. Ezechias enjoyed peace after the defeat of Sennacherib, as the whole world did at the birth of Christ.

2:5.text: Lord. Ezechias, or rather Christ and his Church, invite all to embrace the true faith.

2:6.text: Jacob. Thus the converts address God, (Haydock) or the prophet gives the reasons of the subversion of the ten tribes.
Children. Imitating idolatrous nations, (Calmet) and marrying with them, (Calmet; Septuagint; Theodoret) or even giving way to unnatural sins.
The Jews were not utterly cast off till they had put Christ to death. His Church shall never perish.

2:8.text: Horses. Which the kings were forbidden to multiply, Deuteronomy 17:16. Great riches often precede the ruin of states.

2:9.text: Forgive. Septuagint, "I will not dismiss them." Hebrew, "and thou hast not pardoned them."

2:10.text: Rock. Screen thyself if thou canst. He alludes to the kingdom of Israel, which was ruined by idolatry, ver. 18, 20.

2:13.text: Basan. Israel; or Syria and the Ammonites, (Calmet) whom Nabuchodonosor subdued, five years after he had taken Jerusalem, (Josephus, [Jewish Antiquities?] 10:11.) as the Idumeans, (ver. 14.) Philistines, and Egyptians, (ver. 15.) and Tyrians, (ver. 16.) who felt also the indignation of the Lord, Jeremias 25:15.

2:16.text: Tharsis. In Cilicia, denoting large ships for merchandise.

2:18.text: Destroyed. This was verified by the establishment of Christianity. And by this and other texts of the like nature, the wild system of some modern sectaries is abundantly confuted, who charge the whole Christian Church with worshipping idols, for many ages.
Yea, for above a thousand years, while she still professed the name of Christ.

2:20.text: Bats. The Egyptians adored all sorts of animals.

The mole was much esteemed by magicians, who promised any the art of divination and success, who should eat the heart of one still warm. (Pliny, [Natural History?] 30:3.) The Israelites were always ready to embrace such superstitious practices.

2:22.text: Septuagint omit this sentence, and St. Jerome thinks they did it perhaps for fear of shocking their brethren. In Jeremias xvii.
It is supplied from Aquila's version, "how must he be esteemed?"
Protestants, "for wherein is he to be accounted of?" Jesus will kill the wicked one with the spirit of his mouth, 2 Thessalonians 2:8.
No dependence must be had in man. The Israelites vainly trusted in Egypt.

3:1.text: After the death of Christ, the Jews had none strong.
Strength. Hebrew, "staff," or support (Leviticus 26:26.) in the dreadful famine which fell on Jerusalem, Lamentations 4:5, 10. Who then shall rely on the power of any man?
The Jews were depressed at the sieges of their city, and will be so till the end of the world.

3:2.text: Prophet. Ezechiel was taken away under Jechonias. Other prophets were disregarded, and the cunning man, (ariolus, which may be understood in a good or bad sense. Calmet) every false prophet was silent, when danger threatened.

3:3.text: Countenance. Septuagint, "the admired counsellor," (Haydock) who came into the king's presence.

3:4.text: Effeminate. Hebrew, "babes." Septuagint, "scoffers." Aquila, etc., "changers," (Calmet) who give way to unnatural excesses, Romans 1:27.
Some manifest a prudence beyond their years: but the last kings of Juda did not, 2 Paralipomenon 36:1., and Ecclesiastes 10:16.

3:5.text: People. They were divided, whether they should continue to obey Nabuchodonosor, or listen to the Egyptians. Ismael slew Godolias, Jeremias xli.

3:6.text: Garment. They were ready to follow any, who was not quite destitute, like themselves, Jeremias 39:10.

3:7.src: Plut.[Plutarch?] in Solon.; Pliny, [Natural History?] 16:19.; Calmet
3:7.text: Clothing. The indigent were excluded from dignities, for fear lest they should seek to enrich themselves by unjustifiable means, Exodus 18:22.

3:8.text: For. The prophet tells what will happen.
And their. Septuagint, "are sinful, disbelieving what regards the Lord. Wherefore now their glory is brought low."
They must have followed a very different Hebrew copy from ours.

3:9.text: From their countenance we may judge that they are proud, etc.

3:10.text: Well. Jeremias (xxxix. 11.) was treated by the enemy with great respect. Septuagint, "having said, let us bind the just man, for he is troublesome, (Haydock) or displeasing (Calmet) to us. Hence they," etc. (Haydock) (Wisdom 2:12.) Many of the Fathers quote it thus. But our version agrees very well with the original, as Isaias joins consoling predictions with those which are of a distressing nature.
Yet the Septuagint seem to have thrown light on the Hebrew by supplying an omission from the book of Wisdom.
Thus all must be explained of the wicked, whose malice shall be punished.
He shall. St. Jerome and all versions read, "they shall eat the fruit of their doings, or devices." Fructum adinventionum suarum comedent.
All who hear of this must applaud the just God for acting well in their punishment. According to the Septuagint, Christ and his adversaries are clearly pointed out.

3:12.text: Women. "Let no women be our senate, as the impious Porphyrius objects." The scribes and Pharisees sought for lucre and pleasure. The teacher approved by the Church must excite tears and not laughter; he must correct sinners, and pronounce no one blessed.
The last kings of Juda were real tyrants, and weak as women.
Blessed. Protestants' marginal note, and the text has, "lead thee."

3:16.text: Pace. Protestants, "and making a tinkling with their feet," (Haydock) by means of little rings round their legs. (Calmet) Stridore ad se juvenes vocat.
The daughters of Sion, denote all the cities and villages which were defaced by the Chaldeans, and still more by the Romans, forty years after Christ.

3:18.text: Of shoes. Hebrew, "gold tissue," Psalm 44:14. This term occurs no where else, and many of these superfluous ornaments are not well known. But we may conclude that they are pernicious to a state, and hateful to God.

3:24.text: Stench. The Jews are noted on this account, as if in consequence of this curse, or of their being confined to prisons, etc. Foetentium Judaeorum et tumultuantium saepe taedio percitus.
M. Aurelius "was often weary of the stinking and seditious Jews."

3:25.text: Fairest. They shall not be spared.
"As they have perished by their beauty, their fairest," etc.

3:26.text: Ground. The posture of captives, Lamentations 1:1.

4:1.text: Seven. Many shall sue for a husband, men shall be so scarce. To continue unmarried was reproachful, Deuteronomy 7:14.
After the conversion of the Gentiles, pastors will be much wanted.

4:2.text: Bud. That is, Christ, (Challoner) who was faintly prefigured by Zorobabel, Zacharias 3:8. Our Saviour was the fruit of the earth, and sovereign Lord.

4:3.text: Life. Only the faithful shall be saved.
The Jews, after the captivity, shall be more obedient. But converts to the faith of Christ are styled saints, (Romans 1:7., etc.) such particularly as are predestined to glory, Romans 8:30.
Those who are called to life and the true faith, may forfeit this honour, by their own fault.

4:4.text: Burning. By baptism of water and fire, or of the Holy Ghost.

4:5.text: Protection. God will protect his Church, more than he did the Israelites by the pillar, Exodus 14:20.

5:1.text: My cousin. So the prophet calls Christ, as being of his family and kindred, by descending from the house of David.
Hebrew and Septuagint, "beloved." Dod may also mean a near relation.
Isaias being of the same tribe, sets before us the lamentations of Christ over Jerusalem, Luke 19:41.

The Hebrews had canticles of sorrow, as well as of joy. The prophet thus endeavours to impress more deeply on the minds of the people what he had been saying. The master of the vineyard is God himself, ver. 7.
Hill. Literally, in the horn, the son of oil.
The best vines grew among olive and fig trees.
Septuagint, "in a horn, (mountain) in a fat soil."

5:2.text: Stones. They burn and starve in different seasons, Colossians 12:3.
There was a famous valley of this name, Judges 16:4. The angels guarded the vineyard, in which Abraham, Moses, etc., were found.
Tower. To keep the wine, etc., Matthew 21:33. It denotes the temple, (Calmet) Scriptures, etc.

5:3.text: Judge. God condescends to have his conduct scrutinized, Isaias 41:1.

5:4.text: Was it. "Why has it produced wild grapes, while I looked?" etc.

5:5.text: Down. By the Chaldeans, and after the death of Christ.
When God withdraws his aid, man is unable to stand. Yet he falls by his own fault, which God only permits.

5:6.text: It. During the whole of the captivity, the land might keep its sabbaths, Leviticus 26:34.
The people shall be deprived of saving doctrine.

5:7.text: Israel. This comparison is very common, Psalm 79:9., and Matthew 20:1.
Cry. For vengeance, Jeremias 12:8., and Genesis 4:10., and 18:20.

5:8.text: Even. Septuagint, "to take from your neighbour: shall," etc.

5:9.text: Inhabitant. What will your avarice avail, (Haydock) since you must abandon all?

5:10.text: Thirty. Hebrew, "a chomer shall yield an epha."

5:11.text: To follow. Hebrew, "for shecar," (Calmet) palm wine, (Theodoret) or any inebriating liquor. (St. Jerome in Isaias 28.) Our version is conformable to Aquila and Symmachus.

5:12.text: They are admonished to observe the festivals of the Lord, and not to indulge in riotousness.

5:14.text: Hell. Or the grave, which never says enough, Proverbs 30:15. Isaias alludes to what should happen under Nabuchodonosor, as if it were past.

5:16.text: Justice. All will be taught to adore him.

5:17.text: Strangers. Ammonites, etc., (Calmet) shall occupy part of the land.

5:18.text: Cart. Fatiguing themselves with iniquity, (Wisdom 5:7.; Calmet) and delaying your conversion.

5:19.text: It. The Jews were often guilty of the like insolence, Jeremias 17:15.

5:22.text: Drink. Hebrew, "mix shecar." People generally mixed wine and water. They also strove who could drink most, and the Greeks had a feast for this purpose, (Calmet) which they styled Choas, for the measure which was to be swallowed down.
Cyrus the younger boasted to the Greek ambassadors, that "he could drink and bear more wine than his brother."

5:23.text: Justice. Declaring the righteous guilty, ver. 20.

5:25.text: Still. After the ruin of Jerusalem, the people were led away.
Grievous sins must be severely punished, as was that of the murderers of Christ.

5:26.text: Off. Like a king, leading all his subjects to battle.
Whistle. He alludes to the custom of leading forth bees by music, Isaias 7:18.
Earth. The Chaldeans, (chap. 41:9., and Jeremias 6:22.) and not the Romans, as some would suppose.
Swiftly. Like an eagle, Daniel 7:4., and Jeremias 48:40.

5:27.text: Broken. They shall march incessantly, Ezechiel 26:7., and 30:11.

5:28.text: Hoofs. They were hardened, but not shod.

5:29.text: Lion. Nabuchodonosor is compared to one, ver. 26., and Jeremias 4:7.

6:1.text: Year of the World 3246, Year before Christ 758.
Died. Either a natural (Calmet) or a civil death, by means of the leprosy.
This and the former chapters relate to the commencement of Joathan's reign, whether before or after the death of Ozias.
Many think that this was the first prediction of Isaias.
I saw. By a prophetic vision, as if I had been present at the dedication of the temple, 3 Kings 8:10.
Lord. Not the Father, as some have asserted, but the Son, John 12:40.

6:1.src: Origen; St. Jerome, Trad.; Paralipomenon; Worthington
6:1.text: Neither Moses nor any other saw the substance of God; but only a shadow. Yet Manasses hence took a pretext to have Isaias slain.

6:2.text: The two Seraphims "burning." They are supposed to constitute the highest order of angels, Numbers 21:6.
His. God's or their own face. Hebrew and Septuagint are ambiguous. Out of respect, (Calmet) they looked not at the divine majesty.

6:3.text: Glory. By no means of the Incarnation. The unity and Trinity are insinuated.

6:4.text: Of him. Septuagint, "them," (Haydock) the Seraphim signifying that the veil was removed by the death of Christ, (Theodoret) or that the people should be led into captivity, as a Jew explained it to St. Jerome.

6:5.text: Peace. It is proper for sinners to do so, Ecclesiasticus 15:9. The prophet was grieved that he was unworthy to join in the acclamation of the Seraphim, and had reason to fear death, Genesis 16:13., and Exodus 33:20. He finds himself less able to speak than before, like Moses, Exodus 4:10., and 6:12.

6:6.text: Coal. "Carbuncle," (Septuagint) the word of God, (St. Basil) spirit of prophecy,

6:7.text: Sin. Impediment in speech. All defects were attributed to some sin, (John 9:2.) as Job's friends maintained.

6:8.text: For us. Hence arises a proof of the plurality of persons.
Send me. Thus Isaias was an evangelical and apostolical prophet.

6:10.text: Blind. The prophets are said to do what they denounce.
Septuagint, "heavy or gross is the heart," etc. The authors of the New Testament quote it thus less harshly.
Them. Is God unwilling to heal? Why then does he send his prophet?
He intimates that all the graces offered would be rendered useless by the hardened Jews.
Hebrew may be, "surely they will not see," etc.

6:11.text: Desolate. By means of Nabuchodonosor, (St. Chrysostom) and the Romans, (Eusebius, etc.) or even till the end of the world, their obstinacy will continue.

6:12.text: Earth. After the captivity, the people shall be more docile. But this was more fully verified by the preaching of the gospel.

6:13.text: Tithing. The land shall produce its fruits, and people shall bring their tithes, Ezechiel 20:40. There shall be some left; (chap. 1:9., and 4:3.; Calmet) though only a tenth part will embrace Christianity.
Made. Septuagint, "ravaged." They shall be exposed to many persecutions under Epiphanes, and few shall escape the arms of the Romans, (Calmet) those particularly (Haydock) who shall be a holy seed.
The apostles were of Jewish extraction, (Haydock) and spread the gospel throughout the world.

7:1.text: Year of the World 3262, Year before Christ 742.

Achaz. This must be seventeen years later than the former prediction, 4 Kings 15:37. The kings of Syria and Israel jointly attacked Juda, but were forced to raise the siege of Jerusalem. The next year they came separately, and committed the following ravages. The news of their junction threw all into confusion, ver. 2. Isaias was then sent to inform the king, that the designs of his enemies should not take effect. Yet the two kings obtained each a victory. But they could not dethrone Achaz, as they intended.
Paine traduces this prophecy as a lie, asserting that they succeeded. What! did they make Tabeel king? ver. 6. The Israelites would not even keep the captives who had been taken, 2 Chronicles 28:15.
Achaz had been made captive before. But now the Lord defeated the projects of his enemies, as he will the conspiracy of heretics against his Church.

7:3.text: Jasub. This name was mysterious: Shear-Jashub means "the rest shall return" from Babylon, or be converted under Ezechias, Isaias 10:22.
Protestants, Go "thou, and Shear-Jashub, thy son, at the end of the conduit," etc.

7:4.text: Tails. So he styles the two kings in derision. The distrust of Achaz was punished by the loss of many of his subjects, but he was not dethroned, having engaged the Egyptians and Assyrians to attack his enemies, ver. 17.

7:6.text: Tabeel. Chaldean, "whom we shall think proper." He will not so much as name him.

7:8.text: Rasin. Both the king and his capital shall be ruined.

7:8.src: The year of the world 3327., Usher
7:8.text: And five. Capellus (p. 497.) would read six and five; or, in eleven years time. But (Calmet) Ephraim was led captive twenty-one years after, and the Cutheans took their place when sixty-five years had elapsed.
Most people date from the prophecy of Amos to the ruin of Samaria, just sixty-five years. The former solution seems preferable.

7:9.text: Continue. Septuagint, "and will not understand, even the Lord," etc.
Hebrew, "and since you do not believe," (Calmet) or "because you are not confirmed" by a miracle.

7:11.text: Above. Require it to thunder, (1 Kings 12:17.) or the earth to open, Numbers 16:28.

7:12.text: Lord. He was afraid of being forced to relinquish his evil ways.
Though an idolater, he knew he ought not to tempt God.

7:14.text: Virgin, halma, (Haydock) one secluded from the company of men. Alma in Latin signifies "a holy person," and in Punic "a virgin." The term is never applied to any but "a young virgin." If it meant a young woman, what sort of a sign would this be?

It was indeed above the sagacity of man to declare that the child to be born would be a boy, and live till the kings should be destroyed. But the prophet undoubtedly speaks of Jesus Christ, the wonderful, etc., (chap. 9:5.) as well as of a boy, who should prefigure him, and be an earnest of the speedy destruction of the two kings. He was to be born of Isaias, (chap. 8:4.) and of all the qualities belonging to the true Emmanuel, only that regards him, which intimates that the country should be delivered before he should come to years of discretion, ver. 16.
The Fathers generally apply all to Christ.
Called. Or shall be in effect, Isaias 1:26.
The king hardly trusted in God's mercies, whereupon the incarnation of Christ, etc., is foretold.

7:15.text: The new baptized received some to remind them of innocence.

7:16.text: Good. Being arrived at the age of discretion, Achaz engaged the Assyrians to invade Damascus. Its citizens and four tribes were carried into captivity the year following. Phacee only survived another year, the year of the world 3265. This was a pledge, that what regarded the son of the virgin would also be accomplished.

7:17.text: Assyrians. His aid shall prove the greatest scourge, (2 Paralipomenon 28:20.) while the Idumeans and Philistines shall also ravage the country. (2 Paralipomenon 28:17.) Achaz has vainly trusted in man.

7:18.text: Of Egypt. The Idumeans, etc., dwell on the borders, Isaias 5:26. Yet many explain this of the victories of Nabuchodonosor and Nechas.

7:20.text: Razor. Or cut off with scissors all the hair, as was done with lepers, (Leviticus 14:9.) and Levites, Numbers 8:7. The country shall be pillaged, and all shall be in mourning.
The men shall be despised as no better than women and cowards.

7:22.text: Land. Pastures shall be so large, (Menochius) though uncultivated, the greatest part of the inhabitants being removed.

7:23.text: Pieces. Sicles. This was the price of the best vineyards, Canticle of Canticles 8:2.
Now people may hunt in them.
The subjects of Achaz were much reduced.

7:24.text: Thither. The hedges shall be rooted up (Haydock) or neglected, so that cattle may graze.
Two sorts of mountains are specified; some for vineyards, and others for pasture.

8:1.text: Book. This mystery would require a large explanation.
Write intelligibly. Here all is plain.
Take. Protestants, "concerning Mahershalalchashbaz." Marginal note, "in making speed to the spoil, he hasteneth the prey."
Chashbaz, the son of Isaias, was a sign that Syria and Israel should soon be rendered desolate; and in a more elevated sense, he shewed that Christ should overturn the powers of hell.
The virgin's son [Jesus Christ] took the prey from the devil, who before possessed almost all the world.
Urias. Probably the high priest, who afterwards weakly complied with the king's idolatrous order, 4 Kings 16:10.
Yet at this time, he was a credible witness.
Zacharias. A person to us unknown.

8:3.text: Prophetess. The blessed Virgin [Mary], (St. Chrysostom, etc.) or to his wife. He gives his son a different name from Emmanuel, (chap. 7:14.) that they might not be confounded.

8:4.text: Assyrians. Theglathphalassar, the next year, took the Damascenes to Kir, and Nephthali, Reuben, Gad, and Manasses into captivity. Yet the kingdom continued some time longer. Never was prediction more explicit. Can the pagans produce any thing similar?

8:6.text: Silence. Being willing to receive Tabeel, instead of their lawful prince. Achaz was then terrified, and chose to become tributary, rather than to lose his crown. Herein both offended God, in whom they ought to have trusted; and the auxiliary king looked upon himself as master of the country, 2 Paralipomenon 28:20.
Israel had joined with the Syrian; but was reduced to the state of captivity, while Jerusalem was preserved.

8:7.text: River. Euphrates, (Calmet) with the overflowing of which the Assyrian is compared.

8:8.text: Emmanuel. Christ was born in the country, and Lord of it: though it might be said to belong to the son of Isaias, as being his figure.

8:9.text: Overcome. The defeat of Sennacherib, of the Idumeans, etc., under Ezechias, is intimated, 4 Kings 18:8., and 19:35.

8:10.text: God. Hebrew, "Emmanuel." We have a pledge of God's protection.

8:12.text: Conspiracy. In despair, they wish to submit to the enemy, ver. 6. Isaias exhorts them to have recourse rather to the Lord.

8:14.text: Two. The wicked of both kingdoms, (Haydock) who choose to revolt from God. Many of Israel were led into captivity, and the territory of Juda was laid waste.

8:16.text: Disciples. Let some faithful witnesses keep this prophecy, (Haydock) that when it is verified, all may be convinced.

8:17.text: Jacob. Having resolved on their ruin.

8:18.text: I and my two children. Isaias 7:3., and 8:4.
The actions of some were prophetical, Isaias 20:2., and Osee 12:10.
God announces what will happen, by the names of my children, (Haydock) and by their age, as well as by my mouth.

8:19.text: Seek of pythons. That is, people pretending to tell future things by a prophesying spirit.

Should not the people seek of their God, for the living of the dead? Here is signified, that it is to God we should pray to be directed, and not to seek of the dead, (that is, of fortune-tellers dead in sin) for the health of the living.
Mutter. Literally, "use a shrill note," strident.
So Horace, (1 Sat. viii.) says: Umbrae cum sagana resonarent triste et acutum.
Should. Make this reply: Should, etc.

8:20.text: Law. Sealed, (ver. 16.) or to the law of Moses, Ecclesiasticus 34:28.
Septuagint, "why do they consult the dead concerning the living? For he gave the law to assist us."
Light. They shall die or be miserable.

8:21.text: By it. The word of God.
God. Elohim means also princes or idols.
Whether they seek God unwillingly, or the aid of men, (ver. 22.) they shall perish.

9:1.text: Loaded. Theglathphalassar took away whole tribes, (2 Paralipomenon 5:26.) the year after this. Yet these people were the first enlightened with the rays of the gospel, (Matthew 4:13.) though so much despised, John 7:52.
Here Christ preached first. But after his passion, few Jews believed in him.

9:2.text: Risen. The kingdom of Juda hoped for redress, when they saw the people of Israel humbled, (Haydock) or rather after the defeat of Sennacherib.

9:3.text: And hast. Parkhurst says it should be, "(whom) thou hast not brought up (the Gentiles) with joy they," etc.

The numerous forces of the Assyrians could not save them from the angel. Under Ezechias the people increased. Was not his reign a figure of the Church persecuted and increasing: but on that account, in danger from a relaxation of discipline?
Spoils. They shall return thanks to God for the unexpected liberation.

9:4.text: Oppressor. Who levied taxes for Assyria, 4 Kings 18:7. Sennacherib made war, because Ezechias refused to pay them any longer, and his troops fell upon each other, (Calmet) as the Madianites had done, Judges vii.

9:5.text: Fire. Being cut and useless. See Diss. on the defeat of Sennacherib.

9:6.text: Child. The Messias, whom the son of Isaias prefigured.
Shoulder. Where the badges of royalty were worn.

Counsellor. From whom all good advice proceeds. Grotius falsely translates, "the consulter of the strong God," meaning Ezechias. Though he deemed the Socinians unworthy of the Christian name, (Ep. ad Valleum.) he too often sides with them. Johets always means one who "gives counsel," Isaias 40:13. Ezechias was at this time ten years old, and he did not always take advice, nor was his reign peaceful, etc.

God. The three Greek versions maliciously render El "the strong," though it be uncertain that it ever has that meaning, as it certainly has not when joined with gibbor, "mighty." Why should two terms of the same import be used? The Septuagint copies vary much. Some read only, "he shall be called the angel of the great council, for I will bring peace upon the princes and his health." St. Jerome thinks they were afraid to style the child God. But this reason falls to the ground, as other copies have, (Calmet) after council, "Wonderful, Counsellor, God, the Mighty, the Potent, exousiaszes, the Prince of Peace, the Father of the world to come, for, etc., (7.) His." Grabe (de Vitiis lxx. p. 29.) asserts that the former is the genuine version, and that the inserted titles are a secondary one; so that there must have been two version before the days of Aquila, as the text is thus quoted at large by Clement and St. Iraeneus, the year of the Lord 180; Kennicott adds also by St. Ignatius, the year of the Lord 110.
The omnipotent God became a little child, and without violence subdued the world, which he still governs.

9:7.text: Peace. Christ gives it, and propagates his Church, Hebrews 12:2.

9:8.text: Word. Septuagint, "death." This also agrees with the Hebrew term, and with the context.

9:9.text: Cedars. They speak in a proverbial way, that they will shortly repair the injuries done by the Assyrians depending on king Osee.

9:11.text: Him. Israel. Salmanasar came to ruin the kingdom.

9:12.text: Still. God punishes the impenitent throughout eternity, ver. 12., and Isaias 10:4.

9:14.text: Him. Hebrew, "the branch and the rush."

9:16.text: Headlong. If the blind lead the blind, both fall into the ditch, Matthew 15:14.

9:17.text: Folly. Sin. They are all guilty. He will shew no compassion.

9:18.text: High. All shall witness the fall of Israel, (Calmet) like a forest on fire.

9:19.text: Brother. Civil wars shall rage, 4 Kings xv. Josephus (Jewish Wars vii.) perhaps alluded to this passage, when he said, that an ancient prophecy announced ruin to the Jews, when they should turn their arms against each other.

10:1.text: Injustice. These great ones excite God's indignation.
Jeroboam forbidding any to go to Jerusalem; and the Pharisees establishing their wicked traditions, ruined all.

10:3.text: Afar. When Salmanasar shall come from Ninive to destroy Samaria, to punish the people for their idolatry (Calmet) and oppressions.
Glory. Golden calves, (Osee 8:5., and 10:5.) or possessions, Isaias 9:8.

10:5.text: Woe. Or come on, Heus, though (Calmet) ho is ordinarily rendered, alas! It here indicates that God makes use of this scourge with regret, and will afterwards consign it to the flames.
The prophet speaks of Salmanasar, or of Sennacherib.

10:6.text: Deceitful. Hebrew, "hypocritical," joining my worship with that of idols.
They had solemnly promised to serve the Lord, Exodus 19:8.

10:7.text: So. He will not think that he is executing my vengeance, supposing that he conquers by his own power.

10:9.text: Thus Nabuchodonosor kept the conquered princes for derision, Habacuc 1:10., and Judges 1:7.
Arphad, Arad, or rather Raphanae, Jeremias 49:23.
Damascus. These two cities were not yet subdued.

10:10.text: Idols. He looks upon the true God as no better than any idols, (4 Kings 18:32.) and falsely supposes that the latter were adored in Jerusalem.

10:12.text: Works. Humbling and terrifying Ezechias and his subjects, who were reduced to great distress, in order to avert the impending war.
Eyes. The Assyrians were punished in their turn.

10:14.text: Nest. Some put these words in the mouth of God.
But they shew the insolence of Sennacherib.

10:15.text: Axe. The Assyrian has no right to boast. What can man do without God's assistance?
Gratiae tuae deputo et quaecumque non feci mala.
Sennacherib persecuted the Jews of his own free will, though he was God's instrument.

10:16.text: Fire. The Jews assert, that 185,000 perished by an inward burning, so that only ten men were left, ver. 19.

10:18.text: Flesh. Or body. All shall perish.
Fear. Sennacherib escaped alone, and fell by the sword of his own sons.

10:20.text: Israel now submitted to Ezechias, as their kingdom was overturned in the sixth year of his reign, eight years before Sennacherib's arrival. Isaias speaks of this time, and therefore makes no distinction of the kingdoms. Striketh the Assyrian.

10:22.text: Converted. This was partly verified in the children of Israel who remained after the devastations of the Assyrians, in the time of king Ezechias: and partly in the conversion of a remnant of the Jews to the faith of Christ.
4 Kings 18:3., and Romans 9:27. The apostle follows the Septuagint, (Calmet) "and if the people of Israel be."
Converted. Septuagint, "saved, for perfecting the word and abridging in justice. Because God, the Lord of hosts, will make an abridged word in the universe."
As the apostle has explained this passage, "every other interpretation must cease."
The few who were converted under Ezechias were a figure of those who should embrace the faith of Christ.
Consumption. That is, the number of them cut short, and reduced to few, shall flourish in the abundance of justice.
Hebrew, "the desolation is decreed, justice shall overflow." God will treat all with rigour, Nahum 1:8. The incredulous Jews shall be rejected, ver. 23., and Romans ix.

10:24.text: Egypt. He sent Rabsaces from Lachis, when he set out to meet Tharaca, 4 Kings xix.

10:25.text: Little. Twenty-eight years, (Psalm 89:4.) or he alludes to the destruction which took place in a single night, (Calmet) or in a moment, ver. 16.

10:26.text: And his. Moses thus let loose the waters of the Red Sea on the Egyptians, by stretching forth his rod.

10:27.text: Oil. That is, by the sweet unction of divine mercy.
Chaldean, "before the anointed," in consideration of Ezechias and Isaias. In the higher sense, it denotes the victory of Christ over the devil.

10:28.text: Into Aiath, etc. Here the prophet describes the march of the Assyrians under Sennacherib; and the terror they should carry with them; and how they should suddenly be destroyed.

10:29.text: Lodging. Here, say the Assyrians, we will encamp.

10:32.text: Nobe. He may arrive thither shortly, in the environs of Jerusalem.
Septuagint, "exhort to-day, that they may continue on the road. Comfort with the hand the daughter of Sion, thou rock and hills within Jerusalem."
Hand. As Nicanor did against the temple, 2 Machabees 15:32.

10:33.text: Vessel. Like Gideon, when he attacked Madian, ver. 26., and Judges 7:19. Septuagint, "the nobles."
Hebrew, "their beauty." The empire of Assyria shall presently fall.

11:1.text: Root. Juda shall not be exterminated, like the Assyrians.

Christ shall spring from the blessed Virgin [Mary], (Worthington) for the salvation of mankind. The Jews agree, that this prediction regards the Messias; though some, with Grotius, would explain it literally of Ezechias. They do not reflect that he was now ten years old, and that the prophet speaks of an event which should still take place after he had been a long while upon the throne. If we were to look for any figure of the Messias, to whom this might be applicable, it would be Zorobabel, Zacharias 3:8. But how disproportionate would be the promises to the execution? Some passages may indeed relate to the return of the captives, (ver. 11.) as the people must have a more immediate object, to insure the accomplishment of the more elevated predictions concerning the Messias: but these also refer ultimately to the propagation of the gospel, which the prophet had also in view.

11:2.text: Him. In the form of a dove, John 1:32.
"The whole fountain of the Holy Ghost descending."
Christ was filled with his seven gifts, and of his fullness his servants receive.

Yet all virtues are the gifts of the holy Spirit, and the number seven is not specified in Hebrew, as the same word (Calmet) yirath, is rendered godliness, which (ver. 3.) means, the fear of the Lord.
God enables us to penetrate the difficulties of Scripture, and to act with prudence, etc.

11:3.text: Filled. Hebrew, "breath or smell." So St. Paul says, (2 Corinthians 2:15.) we are the good odour of Christ.
Protestants, "he shall make him of quick understanding (marginal note, smell) in the fear," etc.

11:4.text: Wicked. Antichrist, (2 Thessalonians 2:8.) and all impiety, by means of the apostles.

11:5.text: Reins. He shall possess these virtues, performing his promises with the strictest fidelity.

11:6.text: Wolf. Some explain this of the Millennium.
But the more intelligent understand, that the fiercest nations shall embrace the gospel, and kings obey the pastors of the Church.
Lead. Or "drive," as the word is used by Festus.

11:8.text: Basilisk. Psalm 9:13. The apostles subdued kings and philosophers, without any human advantages.

11:9.text: Kill. The most inveterate pagans, being once converted, entirely alter their manners, Osee 2:18.

11:10.text: Ensign. The cross is the standard of Christians.
Sepulchre. Hebrew, Septuagint, etc., "rest." St. Jerome give the true sense. The holy places have been greatly reverenced, and Christian princes strove for a long time to recover them.
They are respected even by the Turks. Christ's death was ignominious, but his monument was full of glory. Thus the saints begin to shine, where the glory of the wicked ends.

11:11.text: Time. After the deliverance from Sennacherib, they shall return from captivity. Ezechias recalled some few, 2 Paralipomenon 29:9.
Remnant. Some embraced the gospel, Romans 2:2., and Acts 2:41., etc.
Of the Mediterranean sea, and all places to which the Jews went by water.

11:13.text: Away. Under Ezechias the Israelites began to join with Juda. But they did it more cordially after their return from Babylon.

11:14.text: Shoulders. Or confines, Ezechiel 25:9. Ezechias and the Machabees attacked the Philistines.
Septuagint, "and they shall fly on the ships of the strangers; they shall plunder the sea together, and those on the east, and Idumea."
East. Ammonites, etc., often defeated by the Machabees, and probably by Ezechias.

11:15.text: Tongue. Gulf of the Mediterranean, near Pelusium, or the seven mouths of the river Nile. The country was ravaged by Sennacherib, Cambyses, Alex.[Alexander the Great?], and Epiphanes, Isaias 19:4., etc. The Jewish captives shall return thence, Isaias 50:3., and Zacharias 10:10.

11:16.text: Assyrians. They shall march without impediment.

12:1.text: Thanks. Literally, "confess." The Jews thank God for their return, as the Church does for her deliverance from sin.
Canticles were composed on such occasions, Exodus xv.
Angry. They do not thank God on this account; but because he had averted his indignation.

12:3.text: Fountains. Instead of those which your fathers drank in the desert.
You shall have the holy Scriptures, (Haydock) sacraments, etc., John 4:13., and 7:38.

12:6.text: Of thee. He alludes to the name Emmanuel. Christ preached, and his own would not receive him, John 1:11., and 26.
He continues with us, concealed under the sacramental species [of the Eucharist].

13:1.text: Burden. That is, a prophecy against Babylon.
Nimrod began the kingdom, Genesis 10. Belus and Ninus brought it to great eminence. But after 1240 years, Babylon was taken by Cyrus.
Isaias delivered the seven following chapters in the first year of Ezechias, Isaias 14:28.

13:2.text: Mountain of Media, whence Darius came. It was usual to erect a signal, (chap. 30:17., and Jeremias 6:1.) to call troops together.

13:3.text: Sanctioned. The Medes and Persians were appointed by God to punish Babylon.

13:4.text: Kings. Darius styles himself king of the Medes and Persians, Daniel 6:12. Many princes and nations composed his army.

13:5.text: Heaven. Where it seems to touch the horizon. Thus the countries beyond the Euphrates are often designated.

13:6.text: Near. Though one hundred and seventy-two years distant.

13:8.text: Burnt. Black with despair, Nahum 2:10., and Joel 2:6.

13:9.text: Desolate. This was effected in the course of many centuries.
The building of Seleucia caused Babylon to be deserted.
Hence we know not at present where it was situated.

13:10.src: Haydock; Grotius; Matthew 24:27., Apocalypse 6:12., and Jeremias 4:23.
13:10.text: Stars. This is not to be taken literally, but only implies that the people shall be in as much consternation (Calmet) as if the world were at an end, ver. 13.

13:11.text: World. The vices of all nations were concentrated at Babylon.

13:12.text: Precious. Rare, (Worthington) or sought after for destruction, ver. 17.

13:14.text: Land. Baltassar shall be abandoned by his allies. Croesus had been already defeated, before Cyrus invested Babylon.

13:17.text: Medes. Who had set themselves at liberty about twenty years before this. They were not solicitous about gold, Ezechiel 7:19., and Sophonias 1:18.

13:19.text: Gomorrha. Towards the end of the Macedonian empire.
The Persians kept wild beasts in it.
The palace of Nabuchodonosor subsisted in the days of Benjamin, (Calmet) but could not be approached on account of serpents.

13:20.text: Tents. To dwell, (Calmet) or to traffic.
Another city was built, but not so large, nor in the same place.

13:21.text: Serpents. Hebrew ochim. Septuagint, "echo," (Haydock) or "reeds." Babylon was built on a marshy situation, and Cyrus having let out the waters of the Euphrates, they could never be effectually stopped.

13:22.text: Owls. Or jackals, which resemble foxes, and going in packs, will devour the largest creatures.
But St. Jerome explains it of birds, Job 28:7., and Leviticus xiv.
Sirens, fabulously supposed to be sweet singing women with wings.
Thannim denotes some great sea monsters, such as whales or sea calves.

14:1.text: Prolonged. Babylon was taken one hundred and seventy-two years after.
Yet this time is counted short, compared with the monarchy, which had lasted a thousand years.
Ground. Cyrus restored the Jews; yet all did not return at that time.
Stranger. Converts, Esther 8:17. All Idumea received circumcision under Hyrcan.

14:2.text: Place. Cyrus probably granted an escort, as Artaxerxes did, 2 Esdras 2:7.
Servants. They had purchased many slaves, (1 Esdras 2:65.) as some were very rich in captivity, and were treated like other subjects.
Oppressors. Stragglers of the army of Cambyses, etc., though this was chiefly verified under the Machabees, Jeremias 25:14., and 30:16.

14:6.text: Persecuted. The Jews read incorrectly, "is persecuted."

14:7.text: Earth. Subject to, or bordering upon the Assyrian empire. Under Darius the Mede, (the Cyaxares of Xenophon) and Cyrus, the people were little molested.
The neighbouring princes (fir-trees, etc., ver. 8.) were also at rest.

14:9.text: Hell is personified, deriding the Chaldean monarch, Baltassar, who perished the very night after he had profaned the sacred vessels, Daniel 5:3. He probably received only the burial of an ass, ver. 11, 19.

14:12.text: O Lucifer. O day-star. All this, according to the letter, is spoken of the king of Babylon. It may also be applied, in a spiritual sense, to Lucifer, the prince of devils, who was created a bright angel, but fell by pride and rebellion against God.
He fell by pride, as Nabuchodonosor did.
Homer (Iliad xix.) represents the demon of discord hurled down by Jupiter to the miserable region of mortals.

14:13.text: North. And be adored as God in the temple of Jerusalem, Psalm 47:3. The Assyrian and Persian monarchs claimed divine honours, 4 Kings 18:33., and Judith 3:13.

14:15.text: Depth. Hebrew, "sides," (ver. 13.) or holes dug out of a cavern.

14:16.text: Turn. From their respective holes in the monument.

14:19.text: Grave. Strangers seized the crown of Baltassar, and neglected his sepulchre: or if we explain it of Nabuchodonosor, his tomb was probably plundered, (Calmet) as the Persians did not spare that of Belus. In the reign of Alexander, the tombs of the kings were covered with water, and filled with serpents.

14:20.text: Thy. Septuagint, "my." Thou hast been a murderer instead of a shepherd.
Ever. The children and monarchy of Nabuchodonosor presently perished. Evilmerodac and Baltassar reigned but a short time, and left no issue to inherit the throne.

14:22.text: Name. It shall lose all its splendour, and be mentioned only with abhorrence, 1 Peter 5:13.

14:23.text: Besom. Reducing it to a heap of rubbish, (chap. 13:21.; Calmet) as the event shewed.

14:25.text: Sennacherib, (St. Jerome) Cambyses, or Holofernes. The sight of their chastisement would be an earnest of the fall of Babylon.
The allies of Assyria, (Menochius) or the enemies of God's people, will also be punished, Isaias 15.

14:28.text: Year of the World 3277, Year before Christ 727.
Achaz. When Ezechias was just seated on the throne. The preceding and subsequent predictions were then delivered, Isaias 13:20.

14:29.text: Bird. Ezechias will openly attack thee, 4 Kings 18:8.
Protestants, "shall be a fiery flying serpent," (Haydock) like that erected by Moses, Numbers 21:9. Sennacherib and Assaraddon shall lay waste Philistia, ver. 31., and Isaias 20:1.
Though Achaz be dead, Ezechias and Ozias will destroy more of that nation, 4 Kings 18:8., and 2 Paralipomenon xxvi.

14:32.text: Nations. Surprised that Ezechias should escape, while the power of the Philistines was overturned so easily; or when the king sent ambassadors to his allies, to announce the defeat of Sennacherib by the angel. All confessed that this was an effect of the divine protection towards Sion.

15:1.text: Moab. Which would be visited in three years' time (chap. 16:14.) either by Ezechias, or by Sennacherib, though history be silent on this head. The Moabites had been very cruel, Amos 1:and 2:--- Night. Suddenly.
Their misery was so much the greater.

15:2.text: House. Protestants, "he is come up to Baiith," (Haydock) or the royal family is gone to the temple of their idol, Chamos, to lament.

15:4.text: Itself. Every one shall deplore his own distress.

15:5.text: My. A charitable heart will grieve for the misfortune of an enemy.
I shall join in the general lamentations, though Moab has always been so great an enemy of Israel.
Septuagint, "the heart of Moab cries in itself to Segor."
Bars. Princes. Protestants, "his fugitives shall," etc.
Heifer. Strong and ungovernable. Hebrew, "to Heglath and to Shelishia for," etc., though we may as well adhere to the Vulgate, Septuagint, etc.

15:6.text: Nemrim. Or Nemra, (Numbers 32:3.) to the north of Segor.
The country around hence became barren.

15:7.text: Willows. That is, as some say, the waters of Babylon; others render it a valley of the Arabians, (Challoner) or "of crows," to which their bodies will be exposed, Isaias 57:6.

15:8.text: Cry. Of iniquity, or rather of grief.

15:9.text: Dibon. Septuagint, etc., read, "Dimon," which signifies, "blood." I will give it a better claim to this appellation.
Septuagint, "I will bring the Arabs up on Dimon, and will take away the seed of Moab, and Ariel, and the remnant of Adama."

16:1.text: Our Saviour sprung from Ruth, the Moabitess.
The original may insinuate, that the king of the country had neglected to pay the usual tribute to Juda, 4 Kings 3:4.
"Send the lamb to the ruler," etc. (Tournemine.) Amid scenes of distress, the prophet perceives that the Saviour will proceed from one of this nation.

16:2.text: Arnon. They shall not be able to fly over, or to escape the conqueror.

16:3.text: Night. Seek a retreat in the darkest places; or protect Israel when they shall flee before the Assyrians. Their cruelty is thus insinuated, Amos i.

16:4.text: Dust. Theglathphalassar. I need not exhort you to receive my people, as I know your dispositions, and they are out of danger.

16:5.text: Just. This regards Christ, (St. Jerome) prefigured by (Haydock) Ezechias.

16:8.text: Lords. Princes of Jerusalem, (Lamentations 1:1.) or of Assyria.
Sea. Of Sodom, even as far as Jazer, (Haydock) in the tribe of Ruben.

16:9.text: I announce a different sort of music from that which is customary in times of harvest, and of vintage. The liquor shall be tears, Isaias 63:2., and Jeremias 48:32, 33.

16:10.text: Carmel. This name is often taken to signify a fair and fruitful hill or field, such as Mount Carmel is.

16:11.text: Wall. Kir-hareseth, ver. 7. I am grieved at your misfortunes.

16:12.text: Prevail. Chamos shall not be able to help them.

16:13.text: That time. A long while ago, Psalm 92:2.

16:14.text: Not many. It was laid waste in the third year of Ezechias. But its final destruction took place only five years after that of Jerusalem.
The wars against Moab continued three years, after which it was reduced to servitude.

17:1.text: Damascus. When it was taken by Theglathphalassar, or rather by Sennacherib, Isaias 10:8. It was again ruined by Nabuchodonosor, Jeremias 49:24. But after the first taking, it never regained its power. Magni nominis umbra.

17:2.text: Aroer. Chaldean, "abandoned, shall be folds for sheep." Septuagint, "left for ever a resting place for flocks and herds, and none shall pursue." The tribes on the east of the Jordan shall be led captive, as well as those on the west, Ephraim, etc.

17:3.text: Damascus. Their too great union proved their ruin. Sennacherib took Damascus, as Salmanasar had done Samaria.

17:4.text: Lean. All the power of the kingdom shall fail, as in a mortal illness.

17:5.text: Raphaim. Near Jerusalem, 3 Kings 23:13. Septuagint, "of stones."
It will be equally difficult to find any men left in the kingdom of Israel.

17:7.text: Israel. They obeyed the summons of Ezechias and of Josias, (2 Paralipomenon 30:1., and 34:6.) and every after followed the same worship as Juda.

17:9.text: Left. By the Chanaanites, when the children of Israel came into their land.
Their consternation was become proverbial, Josue 2:9., and 5:11.

17:10.text: Good. Septuagint, "fruitless." Israel had abandoned the Lord; and could expect nothing but the fruits of death.

17:11.text: Much. Thou hast laboured earnestly, but reaped no benefit.

17:12.text: Multitude, etc. This and all that follows to the end of the chapter, relates to the Assyrian army under Sennacherib, (Challoner) or rather to that of Israel and its allies.
After the Assyrians had afflicted Israel, they were also punished.

17:14.text: Not be. Phacee and Rasin were presently exterminated.
Spoiled us. The kingdom of Achaz, 4 Kings 17:5.

18:1.text: Cymbal. Or sistrum, commonly used in Egypt. Septuagint, "ship sails."
Ethiopia, or Chus, lying between the Nile (the branches of which are styled rivers) and the Red Sea. He alludes to the kingdom of Tharaca, 4 Kings 19:8.

18:2.text: Ambassadors. Hebrew, "images," (Bochart) in honour of Adonis; (St. Cyril) or rather Ezechias, or Tharaca send to demand troops.
Formed of rushes which grow on the banks of the Nile.
Pieces. With factions after the death of Sabacon, or by the inroads of Sennacherib.
Other. He derides the vanity of the Egyptians.
Expecting the overflowing of the Nile.
Hebrew, "of line," (Calmet) with which they marked out each person's property, after the waters had subsided.
Foot. They worked their dough with their feet, and sent swine to trample on the seed, which required no more cultivation.
Spoiled. The Nile made considerable alterations.

18:4.text: Place. God rules all with ease.
Harvest. The allies shall comfort my people, (Calmet) or Sennacherib shall threaten ruin.
But I will frustrate his evil designs. His army shall perish unexpectedly, ver. 5.
The Egyptians had sent messengers to assure the Israelites that they would come to assist them: but the prophet informs them of their own ruin.

18:6.text: Them. Their bodies shall lie unburied.

18:7.text: Sion. Egypt shall send presents to the Lord, 2 Paralipomenon 32:23.

19:1.text: Egypt. Many refer this to the coming of Christ, (Calmet) at whose presence the idols fell down, and many saints adorned the country.

But the prophet may also literally refer to the wars of the Assyrians against Egypt. Sabacon having retired, after reigning fifty years, Anysis, and afterwards the priest of Sethon, succeeded to the throne. The latter was attacked by Sennacherib. After his death, twelve kingdoms were formed, but Psammitichus reunited them, and had Nechao for his successor.
Behold. The prophet speaks fourteen years before the attack of Sennacherib.
Cloud. Psalm 17:11. Some Fathers explain it of the blessed Virgin [Mary].

19:3.text: Egypt. Septuagint, "of the Egyptians shall be troubled within them."
Soothsayers. Feeble but too common resource of superstitious people!

19:4.text: Masters. Twelve kings. Psammitichus, one of them, shall gain the ascendancy.

19:5.text: Dry. The lakes and the Nile shall not afford sufficient moisture.
If the Nile rose less than twelve or more than sixteen cubits famine ensued.

19:7.text: Fountain. The Nile rises in Ethiopia. But the canals alone were left dry.
Septuagint, "the achi, every green herb along the river, and every," etc.

19:8.text: Fishers. The lake Moeris produced a talent every day for the revenue, and so great was the abundance of fish, that they could hardly be salted. The Nile was also well supplied with fish.

19:10.text: All they. Septuagint, "and all who make strong drink (secer) shall be in sorrow, and shall afflict their souls."
This version is perhaps the best, as the Egyptians used much ale or wine distilled from barley.

19:11.text: Tanis. Or of the twelve kings, ver. 1. They are disconcerted at the approach of Psammitichus, (Calmet) or at the want of water.

19:13.text: Memphis. The seat of many kings, and a very ancient city. Hebrew, "Noph."
Stay. Literally, "angle," denoting the chiefs, or all the land, Judges 20:2.

19:15.text: Back. King and subject are equally confused, Isaias 9:14.

19:17.text: Terror. Hebrew also, "a rejoicing," (St. Jerome) on account of Sennacherib's defeat there, Isaias 18:7.

19:18.text: Chanaan. Hebrew. The Israelites had a connection with Egypt, which the prophets often blame, Isaias 30:2. Ezechias trusted in their aid, when he refused to pay tribute to the Assyrians. Many at that time, or afterwards, retired thither, and served God unmolested, Isaias 11:2., and Jeremias xlii. More established themselves in the country under Alexander [the Great] and the Ptolemies. (3 Machabees viii.) But this prediction was more fully accomplished by the propagation of the Christian religion. Grace no where shone forth more brightly than in this country, once the seat of superstition.
Sun. Hebrew, "of desolation." But the copies have varied. It is supposed to denote the city On, Genesis 41:45.
Prideaux (p. 2. b. 4.) accuses the Jews of willfully corrupting this text in the Septuagint.

19:19.text: Altar. If the Jews were forbidden to have any other than the one at Jerusalem, how can the prophet announce this as a blessing? Onias being excluded from the high priesthood, retired into Egypt, and obtained leave to build the temple Onion, in the Nome, though not in the city of Helipolis, above Bubaste, on the Nile, alleging that Isaias had foretold this event, and that one was already built at Leontopolis.
But we must allow with the fathers and Jews in the days of St. Jerome, that this prediction regarded the Messias, when altars might be lawfully erected in every nation. See Misna, tr. Moneuth, 13:10.
Monument. The cross is set up wherever Christ is adored.
The Egyptians shall embrace Christianity, and St. Anthony of Thebes, etc., shall live a holy (Worthington) and austere life.

19:20.text: Them. The Jews were miraculously rescued from the hands of Philopater, (Josephus, contra Apion ii.) or rather Christians are delivered from sin and Satan.

19:21.text: Egypt. The kings often caused sacrifices to be offered for them; but they were not acceptable, as long as they continued idolaters. The country was converted to Christianity, (Calmet) and the Anchorets performed their vows and penitential exercises, to the admiration of all.

19:22.text: Scourge. By means of Sennacherib, Cambyses, and Ochus. Afterwards the country was quietly subject to the kings of Persia, Alexander the Great, the Ptolemies, and the Romans.

19:24.text: Land. The apostles, who were true Israelites, (Haydock) procured the blessing of faith for these nations, (Calmet) to serve God with concord.

20:1.text: Year of the World 3291, Year before Christ 713.
Year. Eighteen after the preceding predictions.

Sargon. Sennacherib, (St. Jerome) Salmanasar, (Sanctius) or Assaradon, who intended to revenge Sennacherib, and sent his "collector of taxes" to take Azotus from Ezechias, and then to proceed farther.
Psammitichus having obtained the sole dominion of Egypt, besieged Azotus for 29 years.

20:2.text: Sackcloth. The prophets lived in poverty, Zacharias 13:4. Their persons were prophetic. It is not agreed whether Isaias went quite naked, or only without his upper garment. The former supposition would represent better the condition of slaves, (ver. 4.) and is adopted by St. Jerome, etc.
People are said to be naked when they are almost so, 2 Kings vi., and John xxi.
Yet "nothing is more honest than to obey God."

20:3.text: Years. Isaias went so long, or perhaps only three days undressed, Numbers 14:34., and Ezechiel 4:5. Egypt and the Arabian Ethiopia were to be abandoned to the Assyrians, in or during three years.

20:4.text: Shame. Thus captives were generally exposed to sale, Isaias 47:2., and Nahum 3:5.

20:5.text: Glory. The alliance of these nations shall not avail the Jews, who are said to inhabit an island, because they neglected God's service no less than the most distant and abandoned nations.
The changes in empires must convince us to depend only on God, since Damascus and Egypt could not save the Hebrews, nor even themselves.

21:1.text: The desert of the sea. So Babylon is here called, because from a city as full of people as the sea is with water, it was become a desert.
After its fall, it was mostly inundated, Isaias 13:20.
Land. Media and Persia, which lay to the south, and were not so beautiful as the environs of Babylon.

21:2.text: Spoileth. Baltassar is incorrigible, or his opponents must proceed.
Elam; that is, O Persia: (Challoner) Cyrus, and Darius, the Mede.
The former nation was weak, and the latter strong.
Cease. The enemy will shew no pity; nor shall I; as Babylon did not heretofore.

21:3.text: Pain. He bewails the crimes and the fall of Babylon, which at this time was in amity with Ezechias, ver. 10.

21:4.text: Babylon. Protestants, "the night of my pleasure hath he turned into fear unto me." Septuagint, "My soul is turned into fear."

21:5.text: Take up. Hebrew, "anoint." He may also allude to the Babylonians, who were feasting.

21:7.text: Camel. These two riders are the kings of the Persians and Medes.
The sentinel, placed by Isaias, in spirit, or rather by the king of Babylon, brings these tidings.

21:8.text: Cyrus appears like one. Septuagint, "And call Urias to the watch-tower," etc., Isaias 8:2.

21:9.text: Horsemen, drawn by the ass and camel, ver. 7. This was verified long after.

21:10.text: Floor: you who must shortly be reduced to the utmost distress. Baladan was friendly to Ezechias. But Assaradon having seized Babylon, took Manasses prisoner; and the city thenceforward continued to fill up the measure of its sins.

21:11.text: Duma. That is, Idumea, or Edom.
It was a city of that country, twenty miles from Eleutheropolis.
Assaradon desolated Idumea the year following, ver. 16. The Jews absurdly apply to Rome what is said of Edom.

21:12.text: Night. Instead of joy, I must announce dreadful things.

21:13.text: Arabia. This sentence is not in the Roman (Calmet) or Alexandrian Septuagint, (Haydock) and Dedan is a city of Idumea.

21:14.text: Water. To neglect this was to be accessary to another's death, in those dreary regions, Isaias 16:3., and Deuteronomy 23:2.

22:1.text: The valley of vision: Jerusalem. The temple of Jerusalem was built upon Mount Moria, or the mountain of vision. But the city is here called, the valley of vision, either because it was lower than the temple, or because of the low condition to which it was to be reduced, (Challoner) during the captivity.

This prophecy regards the devastation caused by Sennacherib, (St. Jerome) Nabuchodonosor, (Sanctius) the Romans, (Eusebius) or by Assaradon, when he took Manasses, 2 Paralipomenon 33:11., and 4 Kings 21:10.

22:2.text: Battle. He taxes the king with cowardice.

22:4.text: People. He saw this in spirit, though he might not live to witness it.

22:5.text: Searching. That day beheld the Assyrians (Haydock) undermining the wall, and behaving with haughtiness (Calmet) on Mount Sion.

22:6.text: Wall. Arms were frequently hung thereon, Canticle of Canticles 9:4.

22:8.text: Covering. Hebrew masac, (Haydock) "shade," for the convenience of the people, 4 Kings 16:18.
Forest, built by Solomon. Ezechias has also procured store of arms, which were now delivered out to the citizens.

22:9.text: Many, but you have neglected them, (Calmet) till it be too late.
Pool, communicating with Gehon on the west.

22:11.text: Walls. Manasses enclosed the pool within walls, forming a second town, 4 Kings 22:29.
Ago. You have not imitated the piety of Ezechias.

22:13.text: Die. Thus the pagans encouraged themselves to feast.
Ergo vivamus dum licet esse bene.
This conduct betrayed an entire want of faith.
"Nothing offends God so much....as contempt proceeding from despair."

22:14.text: Die. The repentance of Manasses, and the piety of Josias, could not avert the storm. Discite justitiam moniti et non temnere divos.

22:15.text: Temple, in the place of Eliacim. He had been secretary before, (Calmet) and had intruded himself into some office in the temple, which he abused.

22:17.text: Cock. St. Jerome's master assured him that the word which is usually rendered a warrior, has this meaning.
The comparison agrees well with a proud man reduced to misery.
Hebrew, "With the captivity of a man, and he will cover thee." Septuagint, "he will cast out and bruise the man, and will take away thy comely robe, and throw thee into," etc.

22:18.text: Lord; Manasses, who hath exalted thee.

22:20.text: Eliacim, who had been displaced, ver. 15. He acted as regent after the departure of Manasses, who always followed his counsels at his return, Judith 4:5. The priesthood was not then incompatible with civil and military functions.

22:21.text: Girdle, the badge of power, Job 12:18.

22:22.text: Shoulder. Here the marks of dignity were worn. Eliacim was appointed master of the palace, over all the other servants.
Thus we may gather what power Christ conferred on St. Peter, when he gave him the keys of heaven, Matthew 16:19., and Apocalypse 3:7.

22:23.text: Peg, on which whatever is placed shall be secure, 1 Esdras 9:8.

22:24.text: House. He shall be the ornament of the priesthood.
Music. All affairs in church and state shall be at his disposal.

22:25.text: Fall. Sobna shall lose all his employments, and ruin others.

23:1.text: Tyre was destroyed, in part, by Nabuchodonosor. Cyrus permitted all the captives of this, as well as of other countries, to return.
Cethim; Cyrus, or rather Macedonia. Merchants come thence no longer.

23:2.text: Island. Tyre was originally surrounded with water. A communication with the land was made afterwards, Josue 19:29.

23:3.text: Nile. Hebrew Shichor, or "muddy water," designates that river, Josue 13:3.
River. The overflowing of the Nile gave fertility to Egypt, insomuch that Tyre and other nations were supplied by it with corn.

23:4.text: Strength: people who sail. Septuagint, "but the strength....replied: I," etc. Sidon will not be concerned for the fall of her rival. She alleges that she has nothing to do with Tyre. That city would not allow that it was a colony of Sidon, ver. 12.

23:6.text: Seas. The rich Tyrians did so.
Septuagint, "to Carthage." Hebrew Tharsis, in Cilicia.
Hence Nabuchodonosor did not find a sufficient reward, Ezechiel 29:18.

23:7.text: Sojourn. Many fled, others were made captives.

23:8.text: Earth. The merchants were as rich as kings, or the latter sent their merchandise to Tyre, Ezechiel 27:33.

23:10.text: Girdle, fortress; or rather, thou art naked, like a slave, Isaias 20:4.

23:13.text: It. Hebrew adds, "for fishermen." It was formerly covered with water.
Ruin. The fall of Babylon has been denounced, Isaias 13.

23:15.text: King Nabuchodonosor, whose two sons reigned but a short time. The captivity of the people of God began also the year of the world 3398, and ended the year of the world 3468, the first of Cyrus.
Harlot. She shall be re-established, (Calmet) and shall invite people to her markets.
Before Cyrus, she had kings, but they were of small power. The city was become very rich, and well fortified, when Alexander [the Great] was stopped by it for seven months. See Ezechiel 26:14.
Tyre had rejoiced at the misfortunes of the Hebrews, and was punished by the like captivity.

23:17.text: Commit. Septuagint, "be the emporium for all." Great injustice prevailed formerly among merchants, so that it is represented as a dishonest calling.

23:18.text: Sanctified to the Lord. This alludes to the conversion of the Gentiles.
Before, the Tyrians were great enemies to the Jews, 2 Esdras 13:16., and 1 Machabees 5:15. Our Saviour wrought a miracle in favour of one of this country, Matthew 5:22., and Zacharias 8:20.
Continuance. Literally, "old age." Aquila, "with changes of dress."

24:1.text: Earth. After the ten preceding threats, the prophet denounces destruction to the whole world, (Worthington) at the day of judgment; though he may also allude to the desolation of the promised land, as our Saviour joins both in the same prediction, Matthew xxiv.

24:2.text: Priest. All distinctions shall be disregarded.
When Jerusalem was taken, all became captives.

24:4.text: Weakened: Joakim, etc., are made prisoners. The greatest monarchs must come before God's tribunal.

24:6.text: Sin. Towards the end of the world iniquity will abound, and men shall rage against each other, Matthew xxiv.
They will also feel the effects of sin.
Mad: abandoned to their passions, (Deuteronomy 28:28.) excepting only the elect.
Few. The Chaldeans permitted only a few of the poorest sort to remain, 2 Paralipomenon 29:10.

24:10.text: Vanity. Jerusalem, (Calmet) or any other city, will be all in confusion.
In, as was the case in times of mourning, Jeremias 9:21.

24:14.text: Sea. The few elect (ver. 13.) being rescued from the misery of the world, shall praise God.
They are exhorted to lift up their heads, Luke 21:28.

24:15.text: Instruction. The Church is like an island, compared with the rest of the world; or it preaches the gospel to all nations, and to the islands, like Great Britain.
Apostolic men are required to preach incessantly to all sorts of people. Hebrew, "in light;" or Pagnin, "in vales."

24:16.text: I said. The prophet, or any other, may speak thus in the latter days.
Myself. I cannot recount what horrid pains I beheld.

24:17.text: Snare. He alludes to the methods of taking wild beasts, Job 18:11.
Opened, as they were in the days of Noe[Noah].
All sorts of misery hang over us.

24:20.text: Night, unexpectedly, (Haydock) and with the utmost speed.

24:21.text: High. The stars, which in many places of the Scripture are so called. Some commentators explain that these words here signify the demons of the air.
The apostate angels will be judged, 1 Corinthians 6:3., and Matthew 24:29.

24:22.text: Visited. Hence Origen (Prin. 3:6., etc.) took occasion to assert, that the damned would one day be released, though the Scripture so often declares the contrary. The prophet speaks of the future liberation of the Jews; (Calmet) or he intimates that after many days, yea throughout eternity, the reprobate will still be punished.

24:23.text: Blush: be turned into blood, Joel 2:10. Dreadful calamities shall ensue, to usher in the great day of judgment.

25:1.text: Amen. He approves of God's judgments (Haydock) against Jerusalem.

25:2.text: City; Jerusalem, or rather Babylon, (chap. 21.) (Calmet) or every city (Haydock) in the world.

25:3.text: People; the Chaldeans, or their conquerors.

25:4.text: Poor; Juda, whom Nabuchodonosor's fury could not exterminate.

25:5.text: Away. Cyrus (Calmet) shall reduce Babylon the great.

25:6.text: Mountain of Sion, a figure of the Church, and of heaven. The Jews shall feast: yea, some of all nations shall partake of the blessed Eucharist, and obtain heaven. The expressions are too grand for a corruptible feast.
Wine. Literally, "of vintage," (Haydock) on which occasion great rejoicings were made.
In the East, the wines were very thick, Psalm 75:9.
On the rejection of the Jews, the Gentiles were converted.

25:7.text: Tied. He will open their eyes to the truth of the gospel. They shall be no longer as criminals, expecting death, or mourning.

25:8.text: Ever. Hebrew, "he shall swallow up death in victory," 1 Corinthians 15:54. Christ, by dying, conquered death, and rescued us from its power, if we do not voluntarily subject ourselves to it again. This was faintly represented by the liberation of the captives.

25:10.text: Moab. That is, the reprobate, whose eternal punishment, from which they can no way escape, is described under these figures.
The Machabees probably executed this vengeance on Moab, 1 Machabees 5:6.

25:11.text: Hands. All his exertions and fury will prove useless.

26:1.text: Day. Under the law of grace, Christians sing this and such like canticles.
Sion. This word is not in Hebrew, etc., though it be understood.
Other nations have their respective cities. All Christians admit this one.
The captives continue to return thanks. Yet the Holy Ghost speaks chiefly of the Church, and of the general resurrection.

26:2.text: Truth. The Jews who returned from Babylon, were more virtuous than their ancestors, as the prophets intimate; though they have Christians principally in view.

26:3.text: Away: condemning the virtuous, as if they were fools.
Symmachus, "our work, or fiction, is taken away." Hebrew may have other meanings.

26:6.text: Needy. The Jews shall behold the ruin of the city by Cyrus, (Calmet) who was of a contemptible nation.

26:7.text: In. God will remove every obstacle, at their return.

26:10.text: Justice. Clemency would therefore be ill placed. If the Israelites had not been led away captives, would they ever have been reformed?

26:11.text: Not see. Let them perish, or live to witness the glory of the Jews.

26:12.text: Works, both in punishing and rewarding.

26:13.text: Lords of Babylon, (Calmet) and our own passions.

26:14.text: Giants; the proud emperors of Babylon, whom thou wilt destroy. Septuagint, "physicians;" as Rephaim has also this meaning.

26:15.text: Septuagint, "add evils to them, O Lord; add evils to the nobles of the land."
Hebrew may have the same sense.
Ends: princes, or the Chaldeans, sending them also into captivity; or thou hast propagated thy Church over the world.

26:18.text: Wind. Our expectation of aid from others has been disappointed.
Septuagint, "the spirit of thy salvation, which thou hast wrought on the earth. We shall not fall, but the inhabitants of the earth shall fall."
Their copies must have been different from ours.
Fallen. The Chanaanites are left for our trial and punishment.

26:19.text: Dead: a civil death, shall regain their liberty; and those who have left this world in a state of virtue, shall be happy.
Ruin. Cyrus liberated the Jews, having conquered Babylon.

26:20.text: Away, and Cambyses be destroyed, Ezechiel 38:11.

26:21.text: Shall cover her stain no more. This is said with relation to the martyrs, and their happy resurrection.
The blood of the saints shall demand vengeance.

27:1.text: Leviathan. That is, the devil, the great enemy of the people of God. He is called the bar serpent from his strength, and the crooked serpent from his wiles, and the whale of the sea, from the tyranny he exercises in the sea of this world. He was spiritually slain by the death of Christ, when his power was destroyed.
It may also literally refer to Nabuchodonosor, and the king of Egypt, or rather to Cambyses, or Holofernes, but particularly Cambyses.

27:2.text: Vineyard; the Church of Christ, (Challoner) or Judea. It may be the beginning of a noted song.

27:3.text: Drink; or, as the Hebrew may also be rendered, I will continually water it.

27:4.text: In me, against the Church; nor shall I become as a thorn or brier in its regard; or march against it, or set it on fire: but it shall always take fast hold of me, and keep an everlasting peace with me.
God rather speaks of the enemy. If he attempt to lay waste this vineyard, I will chastise him.

27:6.text: Rush in. Some understand this of the enemies of the true Israel, that shall invade it in vain. Others of the spiritual invasion made by the apostles of Christ.
Protestants, "He shall cause them that come of Jacob to take root." Septuagint, "those who come are children of Jacob."

27:7.text: Struck. Hath God punished the carnal persecuting Jews, in proportion to their doings against Christ and his saints?
God punished Israel as a father: but he will destroy the Chaldeans, etc.

27:8.text: Cast off. When the synagogue shall be cast off, thou shalt judge it in measure, and in proportion to its crimes.
The Israelites have been rigorously punished.
He, etc. God hath designed severe punishments in the day of his wrath.

27:9.text: Jacob; viz., of such of them as shall be converted.
Altar, dedicated to idols: then he shall obtain pardon.

27:10.text: City. Jerusalem, (Challoner) or more probably Babylon, of which he is going to speak.

27:11.text: Women. The princes shall be weak and irresolute.
Septuagint, "Hither, ye women, coming from the shew. For it is not an intelligent people."

27:12.text: River Euphrates, even to the Nile.

Nabuchodonosor laid waste all the intermediate countries. Afterwards Cyrus gave the people liberty. On the death of Cambyses, the nations were in consternation; and it was only during the peaceable reign of Darius that Israel returned, though not in a body, as the Jews had done twenty years before, Isaias 26.
By one, into the Church of Christ, John xi.

27:13.text: Trumpet. The preaching of the Gospel for the conversion of the Jews.

28:1.text: Ephraim. That is, the kingdom of the ten tribes.
Flower. The pride of the kingdom shall thus decay.
Head. Samaria, situated on a hill, having under it a most fertile valley.
See Amos 2:6., and 4:2. Samaria was taken in the sixth year of Ezechias.

28:2.text: The Lord. By his instrument, Salmanasar.
Hebrew, "behold the strong one, and the mighty to the Lord, as," etc.

28:4.text: Up. Theglathphalassar was captivated with the beauty of the country, and made it tributary. But Salmanasar, fearing a revolt, destroyed it, 4 Kings 17:4.

28:5.text: People, who returned to the service of God; or it refers to the kingdom of Juda.

28:6.text: Gate. Ezechias reunited the divided kingdoms, and inspired his troops with courage, bringing them back victorious, 2 Paralipomenon 30:1., and 4 Kings 18:7.

28:7.text: These also. The kingdom of Juda.
Ezechias could not correct every abuse; though what is here specified, regards rather the reigns of his successors.

28:8.text: Place. All was defiled: they gloried in their shame.

28:9.text: Breasts? St. Paul seems to allude to this text, 1 Corinthians 3:2.
The abandoned Jews ask contemptuously, if they be to be taught like children?

28:10.text: Command, etc. This is said in the person of the Jews, resisting the repeated commands of God, and still putting him off.
They deride the prophets, speaking words of no meaning, as if their predictions were no better.
Think they that we have to learn the first elements, or to join syllables together?

Why do they not speak plain? Sometimes terrible things are denounced, then subjects of joy! Isaias answers, that since they pretend not to understand, God will lead them into a country where they shall indeed have to learn the language, like children, Isaias 29:11.
Protestants, "for precept must be upon precept....line upon line....here a little, and there a little. For with stammering lips," etc.

28:12.text: Hear. To leave off their wicked practices, and cruelty. The Jews would not understand: therefore Christ spoke to them in parables, Isaias 6:9., and Matthew 13:14., etc.

28:13.text: Taken. God will make his prophets speak, notwithstanding your repugnance; or he will reduce you to the necessity of learning an unknown language.

28:14.text: Men, who make a parade of your knowledge, (Haydock) to turn the most sacred things into ridicule.

28:15.text: Protected. Their conduct spoke this language. They would not fail to make alliances with Egypt, and to trust in idols, whatever the prophets might say to dissuade them.

28:16.text: The Jews and Grotius would apply it to Ezechias. But he was already on the throne, and never could realize these glorious promises. The people were not to believe in him, etc.
Hasten. Let him expect his coming with patience.

It would be delayed some time. Hebrew may also signify "stagger;" (Calmet) in which sense the Septuagint and the authors of the New Testament seem to have taken it. "Whosoever believeth in him, shall not be confounded." See Romans 9:33.
Isaias promises a Redeemer, though these people were unworthy; and then returns to his own times.

28:17.text: Measure. In the days of the Redeemer, they shall lead a virtuous life, (Calmet) or the scorners shall be treated with rigour.
Protection. The wall, (Menochius) or lies, (ver. 15.; Calmet) on which you depend, shall turn to your confusion.

28:19.text: Hear. Under the last kings of Juda, the misery was continual. Captivity opened the eyes of the people, and they were afterwards more docile. The murder of Christ, and the subsequent evils which befell the nation, seem to have had a quite different effect. They will at last submit to his yoke.

28:20.src: Challoner; 1 Corinthians 10:20., and 2 Corinthians 6:14.; St. Jerome, etc.
28:20.text: Straitened. It is too narrow to hold two: God will have the bed of our heart all to himself.
The Jews explain it of the utmost distress, to which the people would be reduced, so that they would not be able to assist a friend, Amos 3:12.

28:21.text: As in, etc. As the Lord fought against the Philistines in Baal Pharasim, (2 Kings v.) and against the Chanaanites, in the valley of Gabaon, Josue 10.
"It is not God's work to ruin what he has created."
He will punish in an extraordinary manner those scoffers, ver. 15., and Numbers 16:29.

28:22.text: Earth. Nabuchodonosor will take a complete and speedy vengeance, Isaias 10:22.

28:24.text: Sow. The works of the husbandman vary, so will God's punishments be inflicted with measure, according to each one's deserts, ver. 27., and Wisdom 6:7.

28:25.text: Septuagint have not expressed all the terms of the original, (Haydock) being perhaps ignorant of their meaning.

28:26.text: God. From him proceeds every useful invention. The pagans attributed the discovery of corn, etc., to their idols.

28:27.text: Saws, or heavy instruments. It would be thus crushed too much.
