28:28.text: But. Septuagint, "it shall be eaten with bread. For I will not be angry with you for ever, nor shall the sounds of my bitter wrath trample upon you."

28:29.text: This also, etc. Such also is the proceeding of the Lord with his land, and the diverse seeds he sows therein.

29:1.text: Ariel. This word signifies the lion of God, and here is taken for the strong city of Jerusalem.
It was destroyed by the Chaldeans, (4 Kings xxv.) and still more by the Romans, 40 years after.
Ezechiel (xliii. 15.) styles the altar of holocausts Ariel.
Took. Septuagint. The Hebrew means also "inhabited."
Sion was called the city of David.
The invasion (Haydock) of Sennacherib is here foretold (Forcr.[Forerius?]) two years before, Isaias 31:10.

29:2.text: Trench. Sennacherib did not besiege the city, Isaias 37:33. But he made preparations for it, and his sentiments are expressed, (Calmet) together with the fatal consequences which he felt, when his army was offered up (Haydock) as a victim on the altar of holocausts, ver. 1.

29:3.text: Circle. Thus provisions were cut off.

29:4.text: The Python. The diviner by a spirit.
Jerusalem shall hardly dare to make a noise.

29:5.text: Away. The numbers, and speedy downfall of the Assyrians, are described.

29:6.text: Thunder. Psalm 75:7. Tharaca was coming to assist Ezechias, Isaias 37:36.

29:9.text: Be, etc. Though God spared the city, for the sake of the good, He will not fail to punish scoffers, in due time, as He now declares.
Drunkenness. You shall suffer for your crimes, (chap. 28:7.) or be affrighted.

29:10.text: Sleep, or compunction, (Romans 11:8.; Calmet) denoting their obstinacy.

The Jews perceived but very imperfectly the meaning of the prophets, when they spoke of a future Redeemer, God and man. They are now more infatuated, (Calmet) having a veil on their hearts, 1 Corinthians 4:3. Both learned and ignorant refuse to believe, excusing themselves, ver. 12.
The more they read the Scriptures, the less do they understand.

29:13.text: Men. Our Saviour applies this to the Jews. The evangelists follow the Septuagint, Matthew 15:8., and Mark 7:6.

"This people approacheth to me, (Grabe adds, with its mouth and with) their lips they honour me, but their heart is far from me. Yet in vain do they honour me, teaching the commands of men and doctrines."
They still continue to corrupt God's word by their false interpretations.

29:14.text: Hid. At the approach of Sennacherib, the politicians were confounded. But the obstinate blindness of the Jews in the midst of such a blaze of predictions, which are evidently accomplished in Jesus Christ, excites admiration. That their ancestors should have found them obscure, is not so wonderful. The prophets foretold this event; and the reprobation of the synagogue, which had been so highly favoured, is a proof of the truth of the Christian religion, 1 Corinthians 1:18.

29:16.text: Not. So it is in vain to think that your hypocrisy or excuses will deceive God, Isaias 28:15.

29:17.text: Charmel. This word signifies a fruitful field.
Shall Carmel be presently a forest or barren mountain? No. But I will work a greater miracle, ver. 18. Jerusalem shall rejoice, and Sennacherib shall be filled with dismay.

29:19.text: Rejoice. Our Saviour alludes to this text, (Luke 7:22.) which, under the figure of the deliverance from captivity, points out the vocation of the Gentiles, Isaias 35:5., and 42:7, 19.

29:20.text: Prevail. Wicked princes, scoffers, etc., (ver. 10., and Isaias 28:7.) shall be exterminated. Ezechias promoted piety with greater zeal after his deliverance.

29:21.text: Gate. False prophets rose up against those who spoke the truth, and condemned them unjustly.

29:23.text: Israel. They shall serve God with fidelity and gratitude, Isaias 30:22.

29:24.text: Murmured. Magicians, (Grotius) or false sages, Isaias 28:9.

30:1.text: Of me. Ezechias was guided by human prudence, in making an alliance with Egypt, though he might have just reasons for refusing to pay tribute to the Assyrians, 4 Kings 18:20.

30:2.text: Down, with presents, ver. 6., and 4 Kings 18:20.

30:3.text: Shame. Egypt had been defeated before Sennacherib's approach.

30:4.text: In the Arabic, Nome, which formed part of (Haydock) the dominions of Tharaca.

30:6.text: Burden. This title seems unnecessary, and may be added by some Jew, (chap. 21:13.) though the Chaldean and others explain it, "They carry on their beasts, presents to the south," to the nations of Arabia and Egypt, infested with lions, etc. The rest of the prophecy is against the Jews, who cannot well be styled beasts of the south.
Basilisk. The ibis devours many serpents on their flight from Arabia into Egypt.

30:7.text: Cried. Hebrew, "called it Rahab, (or pride) it is rest." The people are indolent, though they will make great promises.

30:8.text: Box. This word was covered with wax.
Write, that none may pretend that they were not admonished. Some think that this was addressed to Jeremias: but Isaias spoke to his incredulous countrymen.

30:10.text: See not. Such were the dispositions of their heart, Isaias 28:15.

30:11.text: Us. Mention God no more, or let him not meddle with our affairs. Seek not to reclaim us, we are pleased with our delusion.

30:12.text: Oppression. Literally, "calumny," (Haydock) or rebellion against the Assyrians. This was contrary to the respect due to God's name, used in the ratification of treaties, how wicked soever those princes might be.

30:13.text: For. Psalm 61:3. If God had not miraculously cut off the army of Sennacherib, what would have become of the kingdom of Juda?

30:15.text: Be. Septuagint, "groan," as Origen, etc., read. If you be seriously converted, and trust not in Egypt, you need not fear.

30:16.text: Egypt was famous for horses, Deuteronomy 17:16.
Rebsaces ridicules the Jews for the want of them, 4 Kings 18:23.

30:17.text: Five. A small number shall put you to flight.
Mast, set up after a shipwreck, to warn others, or as a signal, Isaias 33:23.

30:18.text: Wait for him. Having convinced Ezechias that he ought to trust in no other, the Lord rescues him from the hand of Sennacherib.

30:19.text: Weep. The citizens expected certain death, (Haydock) or slavery.

30:20.text: Water. The land will be reduced to a miserable condition by the ravages of Sennacherib.
The following was a sabbatical year, ver. 23., and Isaias 37:30.
Teacher. It seems that Isaias, etc., had been silent. He spoke after Ezechias had sent for him, and God promises that the people shall not be left without guides.
Christ will not abandon his Church.

30:22.text: Garment. Hebrew, "ephod," belonging to the idol, or its priests. Ezechias had prohibited idolatry at first. After his deliverance he was still more zealous, and even those who had formerly retained an affection for idols, saw their vanity, and became sincere.

30:24.text: Floor. They shall not have straw only, but wheat, etc., to denote abundance.

30:25.text: Towers, or chief officers of Sennacherib. All shall be luxuriant.

30:26.text: Sevenfold. Exceedingly great, equal to the light of 49 days.
The fame of Ezechias spread widely. His kingdom was a figure of that of Christ, when this was more perfectly realized, the preaching of the gospel having dispelled the darkness of error.
He alludes to the day of judgment.

30:27.text: Name. Majesty of God, (Calmet) in the future ages.

30:28.text: Error. The unjust government of Sennacherib, who endeavoured to engage all in idolatry.

30:29.text: He may particularly mean that night, when the destroying angel slew the Egyptians.

30:30.text: Stones. The Angel raised the storm, which destroyed many, while the rest in a panic fell upon one another, Isaias 9:5., and 37:36.

30:31.text: Rod. Sennacherib is terrified, who a few days before insulted the living God.

30:32.text: Harps. The sound of thunder will fill the people of Ezechias with joy, while the enemy shall perish irrecoverably.

30:33.text: Topheth. 'Tis the same as Gehenna, and is taken for hell.

The Assyrians perish amid horrid cries (Haydock) and thunders, which resembled the noise made by drums, and by children who were burning in the arms of Moloc, 4 Kings 18:4., and 23:10., and 2 Paralipomenon 29:16. Some think that the carcasses of the Assyrians were to be burnt in this common sewer of Jerusalem. But they were too far distant, Isaias 37:33.

31:1.text: Chariots. He continues to inveigh against this practice, Isaias 30:16.
Salmanasar will ruin the ten tribes.

31:2.text: Words. The Lord will punish the wicked Jews, (Calmet) after the Egyptians.
The former would not believe the prophets.

31:3.text: Hand. If God neglect to support empires, they fall of themselves.

31:4.text: Thereof. He will thence hurl destruction on the distant enemy.
He had destroyed Sennacherib's army, 4 Kings xix. Yet they forgot this and other proofs of God's power and love.

31:5.text: Over. He will protect Jerusalem, notwithstanding the menaces of Sennacherib, as he did his people from the destroying angel.

31:6.text: Revolted. Let your conversion bear proportion (Calmet) with your sins.

31:7.text: Idols. Their worship was afterwards more severely prohibited, Isaias 30:21.

31:8.text: Flee not. Hebrew, "flee to himself," lo. Septuagint and Vulgate have read la, "not." The angel destroyed the army, and the king was slain at his return.
Tributaries. Ninive being afterwards subject to the Chaldeans, etc.

31:9.text: Jerusalem, to protect it, (Calmet) and punish the guilty.

32:1.text: King. Ezechias or Josias, as figures of Jesus Christ, who is meant.
They and their counsellors only foreshewed the advantages derived from Christ and his apostles in a more abundant manner.

Judgment and justice. These words have a higher meaning than what is assigned to them by philosophers. In God, the former implies the preparation of the means for man's redemption, as the latter does the execution; and in man, judgment denotes the selection of what is right, and justice implies the putting it willingly in practice. Thus Christ will fulfill all that he has graciously purposed, with the two other divine persons; and the princes, his pastors, shall discern what is good for their own and people's eternal welfare.

32:2.text: Land. Ezechias and Josias were both a defence to their subjects.

32:3.text: Dim. True prophets shall speak, while false ones shall be silent.

32:4.text: Plain. Some parts of the prediction relate literally to the Old Testament. But this alludes to the New, when the mysteries of religion are clearly confessed in the Catholic Church.
Even the most illiterate are guided with security, if they will but hear the Church.

32:5.text: Deceitful. Hebrew, "miser be called liberal," Luke 22:25. These good princes are contrasted with Achaz, who had oppressed his subjects.

32:7.text: Vessels. Arms, (Calmet) or all the words and actions of the miser are bent on evil.
The ministers of wicked princes resemble them.

32:9.text: Women. Great cities. He announces the impending dangers.

32:10.text: Year. After a long time; or the prophet speaks two years before the arrival of Sennacherib, after the vintage was ended, Isaias 30:20., and 4 Kings 19:29.

32:12.text: Breasts, suckling infants. In mourning, women beat and uncovered their breasts, which, on any other occasion, would have been deemed very indecent.

32:13.text: Up. Being uncultivated for two years. This was still more the case during the captivity.
How. Septuagint, "from every house joy shall be taken away, thou rich city."

32:14.text: Ever. Some palaces had been demolished by Sennacherib, though this seems to refer to the Babylonian captivity.

32:15.text: High, as Ezechiel (xxxvii. 10.) saw the dry bones rise again. Under this idea prosperity is frequently described. The rest of the chapter may very well be explained of the propagation of the gospel.

Forest. Carmel was a fertile spot. Judea shall flourish, and Assyria shall be laid waste. The synagogue will be rejected, while the Gentiles, (Calmet) formerly so barren, shall embrace the faith and true piety.

32:17.text: Peace. The just shall enjoy peace, under Ezechias.

32:19.text: Hail. God's judgment shall overtake Babylon, or rather Ninive.

32:20.text: Waters. Fruitful soils, abounding with cattle.

32:20.src: Clement of Alexandria, Strom. vi.; St. Jerome
32:20.text: Both Jews and Gentiles shall submit to Christ.

33:1.text: Spoilest. This is particularly directed to Sennacherib.
He was a figure of persecutors of the Church, to which many passages here allude.
Remota justitia quid sunt regna nisi magna latrocinia?
Sennacherib plundered Samaria and Juda, and despising God, was himself contemned.

33:2.text: Morning. Speedily. Hebrew, "mornings," or every day. Ezechias thus addresses God.

33:4.text: Them. The neglect of burying these insects has often brought on the plague.

33:6.text: Faith. Sincerity and justice adorn the reigns of Ezechias and Christ.

33:7.text: Without. The people of the country, and the envoys of Ezechias, 4 Kings 18:14. Hebrew, "Behold their Ariel, cried they without," insultingly, (Calmet) pointing at Jerusalem, Isaias 29:1. At which (Haydock) the envoys rent their garments, etc., Isaias 36:22.
Angels. Messengers or deputies sent to negotiate a peace, (Challoner) who wept because they could not obtain it.

33:8.text: Void, though Sennacherib had received what he demanded, 4 Kings 18:14.

33:9.text: Confounded. Its trees were cut down, Isaias 37:24.

33:10.text: Myself, when all human aid fails.

33:11.text: You, Assyrians, are bringing destruction upon yourselves.

33:14.text: They are seriously alarmed at the sight of the fires prepared to burn the dead bodies of the Assyrians, (chap. 30:33.) and begin to think of hell, (Calmet) which their sins deserve.

33:15.text: Blood. Avoiding revenge, and punishing the guilty, without respect to persons. Such was Ezechias, and therefore his enemies could not hurt him.

33:16.text: This was a great advantage in those dry regions.

33:17.text: King Ezechias, or he shall be one of his courtiers.
Off. Their limits shall be extended. Those who believe in Christ, shall cast their eyes up towards their heavenly country, Hebrews 9:13.

33:18.text: Ones. These questions were put by the people, when the enemy approached; or they now rejoice that their severe masters were gone.

33:19.text: Shameless, unjust Assyrians, Luke 18:2. When shame is gone, people give way to every excess.
Profound. Unknown to the Jews, 4 Kings 18:26.

33:20.text: Broken. It was taken 125 years afterwards. The Church remains till the end of time, (Calmet) whereas both the prophets and history assure us, that Jerusalem was subject to destruction.

33:21.text: Of rivers. He speaks of the rivers of endless joys that flow from the throne of God to water the heavenly Jerusalem, where no enemy's ship can come, etc.
God keeps all enemies from Jerusalem, like a deep river.

33:23.text: Thy tacklings. He speaks of the enemies of the Church, under the allegory of a ship that is disabled.
Sennacherib shall attempt invasion no more than a ship without masts would put to sea.

33:24.text: Feeble. All were obliged to collect the plunder, to be afterwards divided. None shall plead illness. The inhabitants of Jerusalem will not feel the effects of sin (Calmet) on this occasion.

34:1.text: Come. Both Gentiles and Jews are admonished of the world's end before judgment.

34:4.text: Away. These strong expressions denote great misery, Isaias 13., and Joel 2:10., and Ezechiel 32:27.

34:4.src: Huet.; Eusebius, praep. 6:11.; Philoo. xxiii.; Pic. Astrol. 8:5.
34:4.text: Some thence foolishly inferred that the destiny of every one might be read in the heavens.
The prophet only means that the heavens shall be devoid of beauty, (Calmet) and covered with darkness.

34:5.text: Heaven. Casting down the rebel angels.
The resolution to destroy the Idumeans, for their cruelty to the Jews, has been taken long ago. All these expressions allude to the last judgment.
Idumea. Under the name of Idumea or Edom, a people that were enemies of the Jews, are here understood the wicked in general, the enemies of God and his Church.
Assaradon fell upon Edom two years after Sennacherib's death.
No strong place like Bosra, shall rescue any from destruction at the last day.

34:7.text: The unicorns. That is, the great and mighty.

34:8.text: Recompenses. When the persecutors of Sion, that is, of the Church, shall receive their reward.
The Idumeans had frequently shown their ill-will towards the Jews, 2 Paralipomenon 28:17., and Amos 1:11.
Sion shall perish as the wicked in hell shall be tormented.

34:9.text: Pitch. The soil was sulphureous, and became neglected, (ver. 10.) like the territory of Sodom. The people are now no more, and only a few miserable Arabs pitch their tents there.

34:11.text: Line. Intimating entire destruction, Lamentations 2:8., and 4 Kings 11:13.
Yet God will not punish more than people deserve.

34:14.text: Lamia. Hebrew lilith. Chaldean, "owl," the bird of Minerva, or the Moon, which the Arabs style Alilat.

Dicodorus (20) relates that Lamia was an African queen, who having last her children, was changed into a beast, and destroyed all the children she could catch; and the Jews deal still more in fables, (Calmet0 asserting that Lilith was the first wife of Adam, etc.

34:15.text: Ericius. Hebrew, "kippoz, (Haydock) hath its nest." It may denote the serpent, acontias.
All this shews the desolation of the country.

34:16.text: The other. All these beasts will be there, (Calmet) or all these declarations will be verified.

35:1.text: Lily. Judea flourishes under Ezechias: but the Church does more so after the coming of Christ, to whom these expressions conduct us.
The Gentiles shall be converted and flourish, as this text shews.

35:2.text: Joy. The primitive Christians rejoiced in a good conscience, and in suffering for the truth.

35:3.text: Knees. Ye prophets, comfort the people with these promises.
The apostles taught the Gentiles to do good.

35:4.text: Recompense. Christ will satisfy the justice of his Father.
He will redeem the world, and refute the false interpretations of the Bible.
He will bring the devil into subjection.

35:6.text: Free. Our Saviour healed both soul and body, (Calmet) shewing by his works (John x.; Worthington) that he had fulfilled this prediction, Luke 7:22., and Matthew 11:5.
Waters. Baptism has sanctified the most wicked.

35:7.text: All shall be in proper order, neither too dry nor too wet.

35:8.text: Way, leading to Jerusalem. Idolaters, etc., shall not be there, Isaias 52:1. This was only verified (Calmet) in the Catholic Church, where, though some wicked may be found, the truth still prevails; and holiness can be obtained no where else.
Fools. The most simple may learn what is necessary for salvation, (Menochius) which will never be done by consulting Scripture alone.

35:9.text: Lion. The devil's power shall be repressed, so that none will be deluded except by their own fault.

35:10.text: Sion. Before the defeat of Sennacherib, the roads were unsafe, Isaias 38:8.

36:1.text: Year of the World 3291, Year before Christ 713.; 4 Kings 18:13.; 2 Paralipomenon xxxii.
And. These four chapters are taken from 4 Kings xviii., etc., as a sort of explanation of what Isaias has been foretelling.
An abridgment also occurs, 2 Paralipomenon xxxii.

36:7.text: Altar. Such is the blindness of infidels, that they confound what is done to destroy idols, with their worship.

37:1.text: Year of the World 3294, Year before Christ 710.
Sackcloth. Emblems of repentance. Sennacherib's boasting (ver. 13.) was chastised, ver. 36.

37:7.text: Spirit. Angel, or a different design, 2 Thessalonians 2:8.

37:18.text: Lands. Hebrew, "all the lands and their land." The parallel text is more correct, "the nations and their land."

37:25.text: Shut, etc. Hebrew matsor, (Haydock) "of Egypt," where Sennacherib had been.

37:29.text: Lips, and treat thee like some ungovernable beast.

37:30.text: Thee. He directeth his speech to Ezechias.

37:35.text: Servant. Hence it plainly appears that God protects the living for the sake of the saints departed. To evade this proof, Protestants (Bible 1603) explain, "for God's promise sake made to David." But God never made any such promise to him; otherwise the city would never have been destroyed.

37:36.text: They. The people of Jerusalem, or rather the soldiers of Ezechias, who saw those who had been slain, near Pelusium.

38:1.text: Year of the World 3291, Year before Christ 713.; 4 Kings xx.

38:9.text: Ezechias. Sanchez groundlessly thinks it was composed by Isaias.
Ezechias was afflicted lest he should give way to dangerous joy.

38:10.text: Hell. Sheol, or Hades, the region of the dead.
He was afraid to die without issue.
Manasses was born three years later.

38:10.src: St. Augustine, de Gen. ad lit. 6:17.; Worthington
38:10.text: The king would naturally have died.

38:11.text: Living. I shall not assist at the festivals of the Lord in the temple.

38:14.text: For me. He represents his disease, as an inexorable creditor.

38:18.text: Truth. He speaks only of the body.

38:22.text: Lord. The answer is given, (4 Kings 20:9.) which seems to evince that this is only an extract.
The prophet prescribed the medicine, and the king asked for a sign before he sung the canticle.

39:1.text: And. Septuagint add, "ambassadors and presents." See 4 Kings 20:12.

39:8.text: Days. He is not unconcerned about his children, but dares not request more. Having given way to immoderate joy and vanity, he was informed that all his treasures should be taken away.

40:1.text: Be. Septuagint, "comfort my people." Let them not be dejected.
The end of the captivity, and still more the coming of the Messias, afford consolation, (Calmet) and to this the prophet chiefly alludes.

40:2.text: Evil. Hebrew and some Latin copies have, "warfare."

40:3.text: God, that he may conduct his people from Babylon.
Yet the prophet speaks chiefly of John the Baptist, (Matthew 3:3.; Calmet) who is evidently foretold.

40:4.text: Plain. For the captives, or the conversion of the world, Baruch 5:6.

40:5.text: Glory. God will rescue his people. Christ will redeem mankind.

40:6.text: Field. On the downfall of the empire of Babylon, the Jews were liberated.

40:9.text: Thou, female. How beautiful are the feet of those who announce good tidings!
Thus a feminine noun is applied to Solomon, Ecclesiastes 1:Prophets make known to all the coming of the Saviour.
Christ preaches from the mountain, and his apostles over the world.

40:10.text: Him. Christ will reward and punish, Jeremias 31:16., and Luke 2:34.

40:11.text: Young, or have lately had young lambs, foetas. Jesus is the good shepherd, John 10:14.

40:12.text: Who. He now proceeds to shew the difference between God and idols.
Fingers, is not expressed in Hebrew, which may denote the epha, Psalm 79:6.
God's power and goodness in the works of the creation, shew what he will do for man.

40:15.text: If all nations be only like a drop, what portion of it do I occupy?

40:18.text: Image. Catholics never pretend to represent the Deity, when they depict the Father as a venerable old man, etc. They do not adore pictures, as our adversaries would insinuate. If we were disposed to cavil, we might bring the same charge against them. For a few weeks ago, "a beautiful altar-piece, painted and presented by the lady of major general Cheney, was put in Hornsea church, representing Christ blessing the bread and wine." But Protestants can confine such things to their proper use, and Catholic must adore them.
"Such things the Jew, Apella, may believe: not I."

40:19.text: Silver. Is God like these idols?
Who knows not that the workman is better than they are?

40:20.text: Wood. Hebrew hamsuccan, (Haydock) which Septuagint, Chaldean, and St. Jerome explain of a sort of wood used for idols. Moderns take it to be "a rich," or rather "a poor man. He who is mean in his offering, chooses wood that," etc.

40:21.text: Beginning, by the light of nature, and (Worthington) has not Moses declared that God alone created the world?
His power and goodness herein convince us that he will not deny grace.

40:22.text: Locusts, compared with the greatest animals.
Nothing. Hebrew, "a curtain." Septuagint, Syriac, "vault, (Calmet) or chamber," kamaran.

40:23.text: Philosophers know nothing independently of God, nor can they subsist without him.

40:26.text: Host of heaven, the stars, etc., Genesis 2:1., and Psalm 146:4.

40:27.text: Judgment, or conduct, (Genesis 40:13.; Calmet) as if God minded not our affairs.

40:31.text: Eagles, who grow young, when they get new feathers, Psalm 102:5.
In this and the following 26 chapters the prophet chiefly comforts his people, as he had rebuked them for their crimes in the first part.

41:1.text: Islands, near Asia, whose conversion Isaias often predicts, (Calmet) as he does that of all nations.
God continues to prove his divinity, (Calmet) expostulating with idolaters, whose conversion is insinuated, and among the rest that of Britain, which is the most renowned island in Europe.

41:2.text: Just one. Septuagint, "justice." Christ, (Eusebius; St. Jerome) Abraham, (Chaldean; Grotius) or Cyrus, whose conquests were effects of God's providence, ver. 10., and Isaias 45.
Kings. Cyrus liberated Persia, conquered Babylon and all Asia.

41:3.text: Feet. His march and victories shall be so rapid, like those of Alexander the Great, Daniel 8:5.

41:4.text: Beginning. Disposing all things, as the conquests of Cyrus, announced so long before by name, evince.
Last. Alpha and Omega, Apocalypse 1:8., and 22:13.

41:5.text: Islands. People of Asia Minor, Genesis 10:5.
The Babylonians made a league with the most potent king Croesus, to oppose the young conqueror.

41:7.text: Moved. Thus the nations conspire against Cyrus (Calmet) and the Messias.

41:10.text: Upheld thee. Cyrus shall gain the victory, and give thee liberty.

41:14.text: Dead. Though you were in the grave, I could protect you. Captivity is often represented under this idea.
You who are despised, fear not, since Christ will protect and reward you.

41:15.text: Chaff. Cambyses perished on the mountains of Judea, (Haydock) and the Machabees gained many victories over the Syrians, Micheas 4:13., and Ezechiel xxxix.
They are here meant, as their exploits resembled those of Cyrus.

41:18.text: Waters. The captives shall be refreshed in the deserts, Isaias 35:6., and 43:20.

41:19.text: The thorn. In Hebrew, the shitta or setim, a tree resembling the whitethorn.
A fragrant shade shall speedily rise up, Baruch 5:6.

41:21.text: Thing. Add "strong," forte, (Haydock) any good proof of idolatry.

41:23.text: Good. The prescience and power of God prove his divinity. Can idols produce any thing similar?
None can tell what will happen, unless God reveal it.

41:24.text: Work. All that you can do or promise. Hebrew, "your work is worse than a viper."

41:25.text: Sun. Cyrus had a Persian for his father, and a Mede for his mother; thus uniting both nations, (Calmet) so that he was styled a mule by an ancient oracle.
These countries lay to the north-east of Judea.

41:26.text: Just, or "the just one," (Haydock) that we may recognise Cyrus at once.
Your words. He addresses the idols. You cannot dive into futurity.

41:28.text: 1:Isaias, (Menochius) or God, (Haydock) pronounces sentence against the speechless idols.

42:1.text: My servant. Christ, who, according to his humanity, is the servant of God, (Challoner) and Redeemer of others; none else being able to satisfy for themselves.
This passage clearly refers to the Messias, (Chaldean; Kimchi) who was prefigured by Cyrus, ver. 6.

It is quoted by St. Matthew 12:18. who has some variations both from the Hebrew and the Septuagint, (Calmet) particularly the first part of ver. 4., which the Septuagint renders, "He shall shine, and shall not be broken."

42:4.text: Islands. Septuagint and St. Matthew, "the Gentiles shall hope in his name."

42:6.text: Gentiles. This was literally verified in Christ. Cyrus is also styled the just, (chap. 41:26.) and gave liberty to many nations.

42:7.text: House. The Jews out of captivity, prefigured the redemption of mankind. These miracles proved that Jesus was the Messias, Luke 7:22.

42:8.text: Things. They shall not partake of my divinity. Our Saviour was truly God, Philippians 2:6.

42:9.text: Them. The completion of former predictions enforces the belief of those which are yet to come.

42:10.text: All. Literally, "its fullness," (Haydock) sailors, (Calmet) and fishes.

42:11.text: Cedar, or the Jews in exile in the desert Arabia, Psalm 119:5.
Petra. A city that gives name to Arabia Petraea.

42:13.text: Enemies. The Chaldeans, (Calmet) by the hand of Cyrus.

42:15.text: Pools. Cyrus deluged the country about Babylon, Isaias 13.; Isaias 21:1.
The proud and covetous, who expected Christ to give them kingdoms, were deceived. He came to teach humility, and to grant eternal rewards.

42:16.text: Blind captives, or converts to Christianity.

42:18.text: Deaf Jews, who would not listen to the prophets, ver. 19.

42:19.text: Sold. Hebrew Cimshullam, (Haydock) the perfect, or favoured. The Turks call "believers" Musselmans.

42:22.text: Men, whom they corrupt by their bad example.
Hebrew, "their young men are in chains," during the last wars, and the captivity of Juda.

42:24.text: We. Septuagint, "they have sinned," which seems preferable.

43:1.text: And. Notwithstanding the sins of his people, God will deliver them, that they may be his witnesses, ver. 10.
Name. As an intimate friend, (Haydock) or slave, Isaias 49:1.
God grants grace without any preceding merit.
Forerius explains this mostly of the Gentile church, though it regards the converted Jews.

43:2.text: Waters. In every emergency, God will protect thee, (Calmet) as he did the three children. [Daniel iii.]
He will suffer none to perish, without their own fault. Hence the Church still continues pure.

43:3.text: Ethiopia. Hebrew, "Chus," the isle of Meroe, (Grotius) or the country bordering on the Red Sea, Isaias 45:14. These were given to indemnify Cyrus. They obtained their liberty to return home, after 40 years, Ezechiel 29:11.

43:5.text: West. The islands beyond the Mediterranean.

43:6.text: South. Egypt. The captives returned from these countries under Cyrus, Alexander the Great, etc., so that before the coming of Christ, Judea was as well peopled as ever.

43:7.text: Calleth. Hebrew, "is called by my name."
My people's chastisements and liberation prove my divine perfections.

43:8.text: Ears. Yet will not hear, (Calmet) or who are miraculously healed.
It seems rather to mean idolatrous nations, (ver. 9.; Calmet) or rebellious Jews.

43:9.text: Former. The pagans were little acquainted with antiquity. Such researches lead to the knowledge of the true religion.
Truth, if they can produce any true prophecy.

43:10.text: Witnesses. The history of the Israelites was sufficient to shew who was God.
Thus the establishment of Christianity manifests its truth.

43:12.text: One idol, to announce what would happen.

43:14.text: Glorying. Septuagint, "shall be bound in ships," to be sent beyond the Caspian Sea.
Cyrus was victorious for the sake of God's people; for he will not neglect his Church.

43:16.text: Waters of the Jordan and the Red Sea, in the latter of which Pharao perished, ver. 17.

43:19.text: New. I shall work the like miracles as were seen in the wilderness.

43:20.text: Chosen. We know not that rivers were found in Arabia. But the people were equally favoured. Christ facilitates the road to heaven by his example and graces, while the most savage tempers are changed in baptism.

43:23.text: Incense. My kindness is gratuitous. In a strange land, thou couldst not offer sacrifice. See Isaias 48:9., and 49:1.

43:24.text: Iniquities. Thou hast shewn the greatest ingratitude.

43:26.text: Thyself. God condescends to act thus with men, Isaias 41:20., and Osee 4:1.

43:27.src: Genebrard, the year of the world 2049; St. Augustine, City of God 16:12.; Calmet; Tirinus
43:27.text: First father. Adam, (Lyranus) or rather Abraham sinned, by diffidence, (Genesis 15:8.; St. Jerome) or was formerly an idolater, Josue 24:2.
Teachers. Literally, "interpreters," (Haydock) Moses and Aaron, Numbers 20:9.
All the patriarchs and teachers sinned, till Christ, the immaculate lamb, appeared. Adam engaged all in guilt.

43:28.text: Profaned, or declared such, (Haydock) Nadab, etc., (Leviticus 10:1.; Calmet) or Moses and Aaron.
Septuagint, "and the princes defiled my holy things."
Slaughter. Hebrew, "anathema." Yet I will re-establish all.

44:3.text: Stock. I will give fresh life to my people, as to the figure of Christ's Church.

44:5.text: Israel. They shall no longer be ashamed of being called Israelites or Christians.

44:8.text: Witnesses. The history of the true religion is its best proof, Isaias 43:9, 10.
Known. Ruled, consequently no other can be truly God.
Idolaters are foolish, trusting in those who cannot announce future events.

44:9.text: Ashamed of the origin and imbecility of their idols.
All this may be turned against heretics, who worship their own fictions; which the Church never does.

44:12.text: File. Hebrew mahatsad, (Haydock; Jeremias 10:3.) "to make an ax, with," etc. This is the remote cause of the idol.

44:13.text: Man. To be styled afterwards a god.

44:18.text: Covered. Septuagint, "darkened." Are Catholics in the same predicament?

44:20.text: Lie. Can I assert in conscience that it is a god?

44:22.text: Return from captivity, (Calmet) and from your former errors, Matthew 11:26.

44:25.text: Mad. That people may be no longer deluded.

44:26.text: Servant. The prophets and Jesus Christ, whose works never fail, Matthew 24:35.
In all this prediction of the Church, Isaias alludes to Jerusalem destroyed, and afterwards rebuilt.

44:27.text: Deep. Babylon, situated amid waters, Isaias 21:1.

44:28.text: Cyrus. This was spoken 110 years before his birth, which shews the prescience and power of God, so as not to injure free-will. The parents of Cyrus could not give him this name to fulfill the prediction, as they knew nothing of it. Amon was apprised that a person called Josias would overturn idolatry; but he had no reason to suppose that it would be his son, 1 Kings 13:2.
My shepherd. Chaldean, "that he shall reign." This was shewn by the Jews to Cyrus, on which account, (Calmet) he gave them leave to return, etc.

The title of shepherd is given to Agamemnon by Homer, and it denotes a good prince, such as historians represent Cyrus to have been. He observed that kings and shepherds had the like duties to perform; (Xenophon viii.) and after his death he was bewailed as a "father."
At first he did not bear the name of Cyrus, (Herodotus 1:113.) which in the Persian language means "the sun."

45:1.text: Anointed, often implies one chosen for some great work. Cyrus was to ruin the empire of Babylon, and to set the nations at liberty. He was a proof of the Deity by executing his decrees.
Cyrus. Some copies of the Septuagint seem to have read kurio, "to the Lord," incorrectly.
Though Cyrus was not anointed, he is styled thus, in allusion to the custom of the Jewish kings.

45:3.text: Treasures. See Pliny, [Natural History?] 33:3. He overcame the rich king of Lydia, etc.

45:4.text: Known me. Before the Jews had shewn Cyrus the prophecies, he did not attribute his success to the Lord, and even afterwards he seems not to have left the superstitions of his country, as his sacrifices to idols are described. He resembled Nabuchodonosor and the philosophers, who did not glorify God according to their knowledge, Daniel 2:47., and Romans 1:21. Cyrus even revoked the decree for building the temple, 1 Esdras 4:5.
He believed there was one God; (1 Esdras i.) yet he did not embrace the truth entirely.

45:7.text: Create evil, etc. The evils of afflictions and punishments, but not the evil of sin.
I afflict and comfort my people.

45:8.text: Saviour. Thus the ancient saints thirsted for the coming of Christ. His figure is styled the just, Isaias 41:2, 25.
Him. Christ, born of the virgin, in time, and of God from all eternity. I have appointed Cyrus to be his precursor, to set the captives free.
He has been spoken of before. But now the prophet turns to Christ alone, who built his Church on a rock.
Cyrus had not a right faith in God, and Zorobabel was himself set free, and was not king.

45:9.text: Samos was famous for its pottery. (Pliny, [Natural History?] 35:12.) Hebrew, "Clay, disputest thou against the potters of the earth?" He shews the folly of idols, after having proved his own divinity.
Protestants, "Let the potsherds strive with the potsherds of the earth."

45:10.text: Forth. As such language would be improper to parents, so it is wrong to complain that God places us in any situation.

45:11.text: To me. Ask what will come to pass, or direct me how to act.

45:13.text: Justice. Cyrus shall punish the Chaldeans and restore the Jews.
The prophet returns to him after having mentioned a greater Saviour, ver. 8.

45:14.text: Stature: the people of Saba were the tallest and best proportioned in Arabia.

Cyrus possessed all these countries. He sent the Egyptians home in the third year of his reign, at Babylon, the year of the world 3470, Ezechiel 29:11. They never became subject to the Jews; but embraced the religion of Christ, acknowledging him for God, ver. 15.

Besides thee. Protestants, "Surely God is in thee, and there is none else, there is no God." Those whom we have hitherto adored, deserve not the name. Vulgate and Septuagint make the people address Christ, the God-man.

45:15.text: Saviour. We confess that thou hast delivered the Jews; or rather, we acknowledge that thou, O Christ, art true God under the veils of thy human nature, and Saviour of all. Cyrus was only a feeble representation of thee.

45:16.text: Confusion. Idolaters shall be confounded, when they shall behold the glory of the elect.

45:18.text: In vain. Hebrew, "to be a chaos," Genesis 8:2.

45:19.text: Earth. The pagan oracles were given chiefly in mountains, where the impostures of the priests might escape detection. They were also generally ambiguous, or mere guesses. The declarations of the true prophets were quite the reverse.

45:20.text: Gentiles: converts, (Haydock) or Jews, returning from Babylon.

45:21.text: Me. He transports his auditors to the times succeeding the captivity, when the completion of the prophecies will be evident.

45:23.text: To me. All that are born belong to me. The Jewish women had seldom recourse to midwives, (Exodus 1:19., and 1 Kings 4:19.; Calmet) no more than the Ethiopians.
Oaths on proper occasions, honour him, Deuteronomy 6:13. Nothing could be spoken more plainly of the Gentiles' conversion.

45:24.text: To me. All that are born belong to me. The Jewish women had seldom recourse to midwives, (Exodus 1:19., and 1 Kings 4:19.; Calmet) no more than the Ethiopians.
Oaths on proper occasions, honour him, Deuteronomy 6:13. Nothing could be spoken more plainly of the Gentiles' conversion.

45:25.text: Empire. Cyrus shall make this confession, (1 Esdras 1:2.) and all who embrace the religion of Christ, shall attribute all their virtue to him.

46:1.text: Bel; perhaps Nimrod, (Calmet) or Saturn, to whom they sacrificed their children.
Nabo, "the oracle" of Belus. The Chaldeans adored statues and beasts. But the Persians worshipped the elements.

Xerxes destroyed the tomb of Belus, after his expedition into Greece. (Arrian vii.) He had there demolished the temples, (Herodotus 8:109.) pretending (Haydock) that "the world is the house of the gods."
Weariness. The priests affected to be weighed down, as if the god were present.

46:2.text: The pagans must have supposed they had one.
Captivity. "There are as many triumphs over the gods as over men."
The former shared the fate of their adorers. Their statues were plundered.

46:3.text: By my. Vulgate may have read a me ab, (Calmet) instead of a meo. Hebrew and Septuagint, "taken from the womb," (Haydock) and treated with the utmost tenderness.

46:11.text: Bird; Cyrus, whose rapid conquests are thus denoted.
He chose a golden eagle, with wings expanded, for his standard.
Christ came from heaven to redeem the world, Psalm 18:6., and Malachias 4:2.
He was the orient, adored by the eastern sages, to whom the prophet refers.

46:12.text: Israel. It shall no longer be a reproach. Cyrus shall restore my people to their own country. But Christ more full accomplished what is here declared respecting the establishment of his Church.

47:1.text: Cyrus overthrew this empire, (Calmet) which now felt its share of misery.

47:2.text: Shame. Hebrew tsammathec, Canticle of Canticles 4:1, 4. Protestants, "thy locks, make bare the legs, uncover the thigh, pass," etc.
Thou shalt be reduced to a state of the most abject slavery, Exodus 11:5., and Supra[Isaias] 3:17., and 20:4. The Barbarians sold their slaves naked.

47:6.text: Polluted; deemed or declared unclean. But thou hast sought to gratify thy vindictive temper, in punishing my people.
The sins of both called down vengeance.

47:7.text: Lady. Pride goes before ruin, Proverbs 16:18.

47:9.text: Two. The empire and the people shall be removed at once.
Enchanters; princes or magicians, who gave them evil counsel, ver. 12.

47:11.text: Know. All this shews the vanity of magic, which cannot announce future events to do any good.

47:13.text: Months, to tell which would prove lucky, Esther 3:7.

47:14.text: Thereat, to warm themselves, (Haydock) or to adore.
In Cappadocia are to be seen "Pyratheia,...in which the magi keep a perpetual fire, and sing hymns about the space of an hour."
These were a sort of open temples.

47:15.text: Merchants. The city was well situated for trade, Isaias 13:20.

48:1.text: Waters; people, (Apocalypse 17:15.; Haydock) or from the stock of Juda, Proverbs 5:15.
He claimed the sovereign power, but had not the fortitude or wisdom of Israel.

48:2.text: City: citizens of Jerusalem, Daniel 3:28., and Matthew 4:5.

48:3.text: Suddenly, when there was no human appearance of the event, as when I foretold the exploits of Cyrus and the ruin of Babylon so long before.

48:6.text: Them. Could you have believed them?
Knowest not. He upbraids their ignorance and indocility.

48:7.text: Knew. Therefore I did not speak of the liberation from Egypt, but from Babylon, which is represented as just taking place.

48:8.text: Opened; docile, (Theodoret) or acquainted with these things. Isaias first made known the captivity of Babylon, and its end; and he insists so much, that people may discern the truth of his predictions, and of religion. No atheist can, with a good conscience, hold out against his arguments, Isaias 40.

48:9.text: Bridle thee, like a headstrong beast, (Calmet) running to its own ruin.
God pardons freely, that people may be saved if they will.

48:10.text: Poverty, at Babylon. I have not treated thee with the utmost rigour, nor attempted to render thee free from every imperfection.
Septuagint, "Lo, I have sold thee, but not for silver; I have snatched thee from the," etc.

48:14.text: Things, as I have done respecting Cyrus? (St. Cyril, etc.) who was a figure of Christ, ver. 15.

48:16.text: Spirit. The Fathers here find the three Persons of the blessed Trinity specified. Isaias was not from the beginning, though the text may also speak of him (Calmet) as he spoke long before the event, by divine inspiration.

48:19.text: Name. The Jews were not forgotten, till they had rejected the Messias.

48:21.text: Out. Their return was facilitated. This may easily be applied (Calmet) to the conversion of the Gentiles.

48:22.text: Peace. Septuagint, "rejoicing," or prosperity for the Chaldeans or wicked Jews, ver. 18.
It is promised only to the penitent.

49:1.text: Give. This fresh discourse continues to Isaias 56:9., relating to the Messias, who is introduced speaking to all the world, Acts 13:47., and 2 Corinthians 6:2. Some apply a part to Cyrus, Isaias, or John the Baptist, as to his figures.
The prophet has foretold the conversion of the Gentiles, as he now does, like an evangelist.
Many Jews will embrace the gospel at the end of time.

49:2.text: Sword, penetrating the very soul, (Hebrews 4:12.) and slaying the wicked, Apocalypse 1:16., and 2 Thessalonians 2:8. Without Christ, his ministers can apply this sword to little purpose. Cyrus cut asunder the bonds of the captives by his decree.
Grotius improperly explains all of Isaias.

49:3.text: Glory. In the Church, God is adored in spirit and truth. Isaias is ordered thus to address the Israelites.

49:4.text: God. He will reward the labours of his ministers, though the people be obstinate. The prophets and our Saviour frequently complain, Isaias 65:2., and Mark 9:18.

49:5.text: Will not. Hebrew, Septuagint, etc., "to be gathered."
The original text, independent of the Masorets, may have the sense of the Vulgate. Yet there are other proofs of the synagogue's rejection.
Protestants, "though Israel be not gathered, yet," etc. Marginal note, or "that Israel may be gathered, and I may," etc.

49:6.text: Earth. St. Paul, explains this of Christ, (Acts xiii.) who said, Go teach all nations, Matthew xxviii. Isaias was one of his first preachers.

49:7.text: Despised, Christ, Cyrus, or Isaias; though it refer chiefly to our Saviour.

49:9.text: Ways. The captives shall find every convenience.

49:12.text: South. Hebrew Sinim; (Haydock) China, (St. Jerome) or rather Sin, or Pelusium, and Sinai, in Egypt and Arabia. Septuagint, "Persians."

49:14.text: Sion, the Jews, who will at last be converted in great numbers.

49:16.text: Hands, which were nailed to the cross. Septuagint, "I have delineated thy walls on my hands, and thou art before me always."
The Assyrians wore such characters on their hands or necks.
Christ will always love his Church, which is of all times and nations.

49:17.text: Of thee. Sanballat, etc., shall yield to Zorobabel, Nehemias, etc.

49:19.text: Inhabitants. The country was better peopled, (Calmet) and Jerusalem enlarged under the Machabees.

49:20.text: Barrenness, of which thou complainest. He alludes to the captives who returned, and to Christian converts.

49:22.text: Shoulders. Thus the Syrians commonly carried children astride.

49:23.text: Nurses. The Persian kings favoured the captives. The greatest monarchs bow before the prelates of the Church, (Menochius) and kiss the Pope's toe. They venerate relics, (Haydock) and greatly enrich the Church.

49:24.text: Strong. Can I force the Babylonians to yield? Surely, and the devil also, Luke 11:21.
Mighty. Hebrew, "just," in lawful war. Septuagint, "unjustly."

49:26.text: Flesh. They shall attack one another.

49:26.src: Berosus, apud Josephus, contra Apion i.

49:26.text: Gobrias and Gadatas betrayed and killed Baltassar.
In their fury they shall tear their own flesh.

50:1.text: Away. Such a one could not be received again, if she had taken another husband, Deuteronomy 24:3. Some explain this of the captives. But God restored them to favour. It seems rather to relate to the reprobation (Calmet) of the synagogue, which will never again become the true Church, (Haydock) though many of Israel will be converted, Romans 11:25.

Sold you, as a father might do, Exodus 21:1., and Matthew 18:15. St. Ambrose (Tob.[Tobias?] viii.) inveighs against such cruel parents, as the Christian religion had not then entirely repressed this inhumanity.
God rejected the synagogue, not out of hard-heartedness or want, but because of her sins.

50:2.text: Hear. My spouse had gone after other lovers. The people refused to hear the prophets; and the priests were become as corrupt as the rest, when the city was taken by the Chaldeans and by the Romans.
I could work the same miracles, as I did when Israel came out of Egypt.

50:4.text: Weary. Isaias speaks in the name of Christ, whose words carried conviction and comfort along with them, John 6:69., and 7:46.
Christ preached more powerfully than Isaias, and continues to do so by his pastors.

50:6.text: Spit. The greatest indignity, Job 30:10., and Deuteronomy 25:9. Yet this was the treatment of our Saviour, Luke 18:31., and Matthew 26:67.
"The great Grotius, (I wish he were great in explaining the prophets)" applies this to Jeremias.

50:7.text: Rock. Christ heard the accusations of his enemies unmoved, as he had not been afraid to blame the conduct of the Pharisees.

50:10.text: Light. The faithful are exhorted to take courage, while the Romans will destroy the rebellious Jews, (ver. 11.; Calmet) and the wicked shall dwell in hell fire.

51:1.text: Lord. He speaks of the redemption of mankind, under the allegory of the return from captivity.

51:2.text: Alone, and gave him children when he was grown old, and Sara barren. I can surely save you likewise.
The example of these great progenitors is set before the Jews. Thus St. Paul says: Remember your prelates, Hebrews xiii.

51:4.text: Nations. This was verified when the Bible was translated into Greek, and still more by the preaching of the gospel.

51:5.text: Just. Christ, prefigured by Cyrus, whose empire was very mild, Isaias 44:28.

51:6.text: Fail. Matthew 24:35. The prosperity of the Jews was not of long duration. But the Church will remain till the end of time.

51:9.text: Proud. Hebrew, "Rahab," Pharao, king of Egypt, Job 26:12., and Ezechiel 29:3.
God drowned in the water, which had retired for Israel. He will thus destroy the devil.

51:13.text: Oppressor. If thou hadst not abandoned God, thou wouldst have had no reason to fear Nabuchodonosor; and his power is now gone.

51:14.text: Fail. Cyrus shall quickly restore the Jews to their country. He shall not lay waste the provinces, like many conquerors.

51:16.text: People. This may refer to Isaias, Cyrus, etc., as figures of Christ, Isaias 49:1. He has bestowed greater favours on Christians than Cyrus did on the Jews, opening the kingdom of heaven to true believers, etc., Ephesians 2:19.

51:17.text: Dregs. Take courage: Babylon's turn is come, ver. 23.

51:19.text: Two. War and famine cause desolation and destruction.
Jerusalem was reduced to the greatest misery in the last siege under Nabuchodonosor, Lamentations 4:3.

51:20.text: Many accounts respecting it are fabulous. Some understand a sort of wolf, mentioned by Pliny, [Natural History?] 8:34. Septuagint, "like beet half boiled."

51:23.text: Over. This inhumanity was not uncommon, Josue 10:24., and Psalm 109:1.

52:1.text: Thee. Judea was no more laid waste by its ancient enemies. The persecution of Epiphanes did not continue long. Many have attacked the Church; but they cannot overcome her, nor will she cease to preach the truth, and to continue pure and holy.

52:3.text: Money. The Chaldeans acted impolitically in leaving the country without inhabitants, and Cyrus will derive no immediate advantage from your return.
The Jews had not injured the Chaldeans, nor mankind the devil. But both had offended God, who out of mercy paid their ransom.

52:4.text: Assyrian. Pharao, (Sa; Tirinus) or rather Nabuchodonosor, (Calmet) and the princes of Assyria, who acted tyrannically.

52:5.text: Long, by the Chaldeans, weak Jews, and strangers, who misconstrue my conduct towards my people, and represent it as the effect of injustice, or of weakness.

52:6.text: Here. Jesus Christ appears, the Redeemer foretold so long before.

52:7.text: Peace. He comes like a conqueror to save his people. It may also be applied to the prophets and apostles, (Calmet) as St. Paul explains it, Romans 10:15.

52:8.text: Watchmen, prophets. The angels sung at the birth of Christ, Luke 2:14.

52:11.text: Lord, the Levites departing from Babylon, 1 Esdras 1:7., and Numbers 3:6., and 4:5., etc.
St. Paul proves hence that communication with infidels in spiritual things is always unlawful, 2 Corinthians 6:17.

52:13.text: Servant, Christ. In vain do the Jews attempt to apply this to any other. It is wonderful that Grotius should follow their steps, and understand Jeremias to be meant; though elsewhere he allows that the prophet speaks only of the Messias.

This author [Grotius] has been of great prejudice to religion. The Chaldean and many modern Jews have been compelled by evidence to apply this to the Messias. See Geir., etc. Jesus was pleased to assume the form of a servant, Philippians 2:7.

52:14.text: Of men, who have disfigured the countenance of our Saviour with buffets, etc. The Jews would not receive him under this abject form, though it had been foretold equally with his elevation.

52:15.text: Sprinkle with baptism, (Haydock) and his manifold graces.
Septuagint, "so many nations shall wonder at him."
Mouth, out of reverence. How many great princes have submitted to his yoke?
Beheld. The Gentiles (Menochius) embrace the faith, at the sight of Christ's miracles.

53:1.text: Revealed. Who could have believed such things? The apostles complain how few were converted, John 12:38., and Romans 10:16.
These would not submit, though the gospel was not against reason.

53:2.text: Was. Septuagint, "he had no appearance nor beauty. But his appearance was abject and deficient above all men; a," etc.
That we. Literally, "and we have desired him." Notwithstanding his abject condition, He was the desired of all nations, and by his wounds we are healed.

Some assert that the person of Christ was not beautiful, while others think that his wounds prevented it from being discerned. Salmeron would supply a negation from the first number: "We have not desired him."

53:3.text: Not. The whole life of Christ was spent in the midst of poverty, and contradictions, Hebrews 4:15. He has thus taught us to despise ourselves.

53:4.text: Sorrows. Healing them by his own afflictions, Matthew 8:15. Sickness is an effect of sin, which Jesus came to destroy, 1 Peter 2:24
Leper, who was bound to have his face covered, ver. 3., and Leviticus 13:45.
God. Payva (Def. Trin. iv.) assures us that many Jews were converted by the perusal of this chapter, and particularly of this verse, which may be rendered "as a God wounded and afflicted."

53:5.text: Healed. He inculcates this important truth repeatedly. Christ nailed the hand-writing that was against us to the cross, Colossians 2:14.

53:6.text: Astray. We belong to his fold, Isaias 40:11., John 10:11., and Luke 15:4.--- Laid. Septuagint, "abandoned him to our sins," as to so many executioners; (Calmet) "and he, because he had been abused, opened," etc.

53:7.text: Will. The pagans were very attentive that the victim should not make much resistance.
Our Saviour offers himself willingly, knowing the inefficacy of legal victims, Psalm 39:7.

53:8.text: Judgment, or by an unjust and cruel judgment.

Septuagint, "in humiliation, (Haydock) or humility, his judgment was taken away," or rescinded, by his glorious resurrection. St. Philip follows this version in explaining this passage to the eunuch, Acts 8:33.
Generation, from his eternal Father or from the Virgin [Mary], his incarnation, life, resurrection, or posterity in the Church. All these may be meant, and are inexplicable.

53:9.text: Death. Hebrew, "and he made his grave with the wicked men, and with the rich man, in his death."

Grave and death seem to be transposed; and we might better read, "He was taken up with wicked men in his death, and with a rich man was his sepulchre." This indeed is only a conjecture, but well grounded in the context. See Josue 24:19.
Septuagint, "and I will give the wicked for his grave, and the rich men for his death."
The rich man may denote the small number of Jews who embraced the faith.

They esteemed themselves rich, and were highly favoured by God; yet they were blinded, (Haydock) and given up to the Romans, in punishment of their deicide, Matthew 27:25. The Church is gathered both from Jews and Gentiles, ver. 10.
"He will send to hell the wicked," (Chaldean) who slew him.
Christ was buried where malefactors were generally, yet honourably, in the tomb of Joseph [of Arimathea].

53:10.text: Bruise. Septuagint, "to cleanse him from the wound."
God was pleased that he should satisfy for our crimes.
Hand. Christ has died for all, and established a Church which shall not perish.

53:12.text: Many. Even to the ends of the earth, Psalm 2:8.
Strong. Demons, Jews, etc. Hebrew, "with the strong" apostles.
Transgressors. His executioners. The gospel could not speak plainer.

54:1.text: Barren Jews in captivity, or Church of the Gentiles, to which alone many of the expressions can be applied.
The Gentiles were before unfruitful, as the Jews will be till towards the latter times.

54:2.text: Stakes, to receive so great a family. All the Israelites did not return, and it does not appear that many embraced their religion, as they have done that of Christ.

54:3.text: Left. To the north and south. Jerusalem increased. But what was it compared with the Christian establishment!

54:4.text: Widowhood. Thy former excesses shall be forgotten.

54:5.text: Thee. Hebrew, "Bohalaic (Haydock) shall be your Baalim," or husband, who was styled Lord, 1 Peter 3:6. Perhaps he may allude to the two wives, the synagogue and the Church, or to the idols, which should be adored no more.

54:6.text: Youth. This enhances her fault. God is pleased to overlook it, in the captives (chap. 50:1.) and Gentiles.

54:9.text: Earth. Giving him the rainbow for a sign. My covenant with the Church is equally irrevocable: she is founded on a rock, Matthew 16:18.
Christ will no more abandon her than he will drown the world. Some mountains shall be moved out of their place, but she shall not.

54:11.text: Sapphires. Hebrew, "antimony," a mineral shining like silver, 4 Kings 9:30.

54:12.text: Bulwarks. Hebrew, windows of crystal; (Ezechiel 27:1[].; Calmet) Protestants, "of agate."
All this is allegorical, like the redemption of the new Jerusalem, Apocalypse. xxi.

54:14.text: Thee. The Cutheans rendered the Jews suspected, 1 Esdras 4:2., and 6:1

54:15.text: To thee, in the inheritance. This was verified in the Church. We have no accout of many being converted before.

54:16.text: Destroy. I can give peace or war.
Septuagint, "but I have created thee not for utter destruction."

55:1.text: Waters, which in that country are very scarce.
In the western Churches, wine and honey were given to the new baptized, Isaias 7:15.
Christ invites all to come to him, John 4:14,. and 7:37. The establishment of the Church is described under the figure of the return from Babylon.
Grace is offered to all. But only those are justified who thirst, and do their best, Matthew 5:6.

55:3.text: David. I will be equally faithful to you, Psalm 88:29. Septuagint, "the holy things of David faithful," Acts 13:34. The resurrection of Christ fully accomplished the promise made to David.

55:4.text: Him. David, who continually proclaimed the divine mercies, (Psalm 17:1.) or rather Christ; though Grotius alone would refer it to Jeremias.

55:6.text: Near. He will shortly (Calmet) turn to the Gentiles, John 12:35., and Acts 13:46.

55:7.text: Way. This is a necessary preliminary to God's service.

55:8.text: My ways. I am not vindictive, but require a sincere conversion.
We cannot serve both God and the world. We must therefore adhere to the former.

55:11.text: Sent it. I will assuredly bring you from Babylon; and the rain shall sooner return upwards than I will break my promise.

55:12.text: Ipsa sonant arbusta Deus Deus ille, Menacla.

55:13.text: Myrtle-tree. Instead of the wicked, the just shall be seen.
Away. The conversion and sanctity of the Gentiles shall be a trophy to the Lord.

56:1.text: Judgment, the right resolution to do God's will, which justice executes, Isaias 32.
My justice. Septuagint, "mercy." Christ is at hand. Prepare for your deliverance, by keeping the commandments.

56:2.text: Sabbath. All the Jewish festivals, (Calmet) as well as those of the Christian Church, (Haydock) and the whole law.

56:3.text: People. The Jews would not admit all nations to their communion, Deuteronomy 33:1. A little before Christ's coming, they began to make more proselytes; (Matthew 23:15.) and the sacred books being translated, came to the knowledge of the Gentiles, who were thus induced by degrees to embrace the true faith. The road to heaven was always open for those who kept the commandments, (ver. 6.; Calmet) though they might not receive circumcision.
Christ has removed the wall of separation, (Ephesians 2:14.; Calmet) and established one fold for all, John 10.

56:4.text: Eunuchs. It was ignominious to have no children among the Jews, as the propagation of the true religion depended much on their numbers. But now, since the Church is gathered from all nations, virginity is preferable to marriage, and those who keep the sabbath, or all the commandments, and choose this state freely, will receive greater glory than the other sons and daughters of God. Against this plain meaning, P. Martyr (de Coelib.) asserts that God prefers eunuchs only before those who transgress the law. But he gives them a place better than his other sons, etc.! (Protestants Bible, 1603) understand that eunuchs shall be called after (or according to) God's people, and be of the same religion, which implies no preference at all. They add, therefore, yea, under Christ the dignity of the faithful shall be greater than the Jews were at that time; as if the comparison were between God's servants before and after Christ, and not between eunuchs and such as have children. How much better is it for us to follow the holy Fathers, who hence commend those who make a vow of perpetual chastity? They shall possess an excellent dignity among the angels.
The rewards of continency are great, eximia.
"In the eternal mansion they are preferred before children."

Such spiritual eunuchs, as St. John the evangelist, are meant. "He hath chosen what the Lord would, that he should offer more than was commanded....He who is an eunuch, and performs all that is prescribed, shall have....the best place, so that he shall be a tower, and occupy the rank of a priest, and instead of children of the flesh, shall have many spiritual children."

The law excluded eunuchs from the Church. (Deuteronomy 23:1.) But under the gospel, they may enter heaven. (Matthew 19., and 1 Corinthians 7:32., etc.) Daniel and his companions were eunuchs, yet in high estimation; and virtuous eunuchs are commended.
Choose. Observing the commandments and counsels, like religious men.
Those who choose to do more than is commanded, will have a greater reward.

56:7.text: Prayer. So the temple is justly styled.

This shall be open to all nations. After the captivity, the Jews condescended to let the Gentiles have a court, and they even suffered some princes to go into the court of the priests, 2 Machabees 3:33. Physcon wished to penetrate into the inner sanctuary, (3 Machabees; Ecclesiasticus l.) which could not be granted.

56:9.text: Beasts. Here a fresh discourse begins to Isaias 61. The Chaldeans and Romans are invited to punish God's people for their sins, committed before the captivity, Zacharias 14:2.
The prophet foresees the negligence of some pastors, and denounces their rigorous chastisement.

56:10.text: We know (Haydock) only Jeremias who continued firm, Lamentations 2:14. In the days of Christ, the corruption was not diminished.

56:11.text: Last. The scribes devour the houses of widows, making long prayers, Matthew 23:14. They are blind, Matthew 15:14.

57:1.text: The just. Christ, (Calmet) Josias, (Grotius) or any whose cause is just, yet finds no protection from such corrupt magistrates.
Evil, by the wicked, or to prevent his fall, 4 Kings 12:20. People little consider what a loss the world sustains, when those die who might have averted the divine wrath.
They are usually taken away, that they may not witness such misfortunes, and are settled in eternal peace.

57:2.text: Bed. The grave which affords rest to the virtuous, Josias, etc.

57:4.text: Tongue, in contempt. Saints, and particularly Jesus Christ, have been exposed to ridicule.

57:5.text: Comfort. Hebrew, "heat," abandoning yourselves to shameful excesses.
Torrents, to avoid being seen. Such sacrifices would have been incredible, if the Scriptures, and all history did not prove their existence, Deuteronomy 12:31., and Wisdom 12:3., and 4 Kings 23:10.

57:6.text: Them. The stones of the torrent, which were often the objects of adoration, Leviticus 26:1., and Genesis 28:18. The god, Helagabalus, was a rough boundary stone.

57:7.text: Bed, like a shameless prostitute. The idols are generally represented in this light, as corrupting God's people.

57:8.text: Remembrance. Domestic gods. (St. Jerome) The Lares or Penates were usually placed in the court or porch. The Jews probably used Hecate or Trivia, for the same purpose, Isaias 65:11., and 66:17. To prevent this impiety, God had ordered some of the law to be written on the doors, Deuteronomy 6:9. But this it seems was disregarded, 4 Kings 23:8., and 1 Machabees 1:58., and Ezechiel 8:5.
Near me. Idols were placed in the very temple, Jeremias 35:15., and 4 Kings 21:4., and Ezechiel 8:3., and 16:17.
Hand. Protestants, "where thou sawest it." Thou didst even invite thy lovers by presents, Ezechiel 16:32.

57:9.text: King. Moloc, or (Calmet) any foreign king, of whose alliance God did not approve.
To please them, the true religion was adulterated.

57:10.text: Rest. They were obstinate before the coming of Nabuchodonosor, and of the Romans.
Asked. Confiding in their own strength.

57:11.text: Afraid, since thou hast despised me, my laws and offers.

57:13.text: Companies, or princes, in whom thou hast confided. Assyria and Egypt cannot save themselves.

57:14.text: And 1:Seeing there is no aid in man, God will save his people for his own goodness' sake.

57:16.text: End. I will not always threaten or be angry, Genesis 6:3.
God spares the humble penitent, and grants what they desire with as much eagerness as a sailor does a fair wind.
He does not regard the indifferent.

57:17.text: Heart. Dreadful state of the abandoned sinner!

57:19.text: Lips. Whatever they could ask, so that they might sing canticles. All shall be content. He alludes to the liberation of the captives, which was near, and to the redemption of mankind far off.

57:20.text: The works of the wicked are fruitless. They have no content. (Calmet) Non enim gazae neque consularis Summovet lictor miseros tumultus, Mentis et curas laqueata circum, Tecta volantes. -----
The obstinate sinner can receive no pardon.

58:1.text: Some will not hear, and those must be rebuked with all patience, till they follow virtue.

58:2.text: Approach, and contend with God, scrutinizing his conduct, (Proverbs 25:27.) and doing good for the sake of applause and self-interest.

58:3.text: Will. This alone suggested their fasts, and they did not shew compassion, Ezechiel 7:2.
Debtors, who are not able to pay.

58:4.text: Strife. The usual works were interrupted. The Church formerly forbade law-suits on fast-days.

58:5.text: Circle. They affected extreme debility, Matthew 6:16.
Ashes. These external marks of penance are not condemned, but the want of corresponding sentiments.

Protestants would hence infer that fasting from flesh is not requisite, or a religious worship. But St. Jerome shews the contrary, provided it be joined with the observance of other commandments, as the saints and Christ himself have shewn us.

58:7.text: Thin cakes are still used in the East.

58:8.text: Health. Aquila, "the scar of thy wound shall soon be covered."
Up. He shall close the rear, like the angel in the cloud, Exodus 13:21., and 14:19. He will grant thee rest from bondage in the grave and in heaven.

58:9.text: Some explain it of the ordaining sacred ministers, or taking another's property.

58:10.text: Soul, effectually, and with love relieving the distressed.

58:11.text: Fail. Alexandrian Septuagint adds, "and thy bones as a flower shall spring and grow fat, and shall inherit ages of ages." St. Jerome says this is not in the best copies.

58:12.text: Generation. As the Jews did not comply with the condition, the Church falls heir to these promises.

58:13.text: Sabbath, doing no work, or refraining from the violation of festivals.
Delightful. We must not think the sabbath of the Lord a loss: (Amos 8:5.) but rejoice in praising him, Psalm 45:11.
A word, or to apply to God's word.
Pious reading on holidays is the duty of all who have an opportunity.

58:14.text: Septuagint, "upon the good things of the land."

59:2.text: Iniquities. The history of Susanna shews that the captives were not all free from sin, which alone prevented their liberation, Lamentations 3:44. (Calmet) [Daniel xiii.]
God is willing and able to save. He punishes for sin, to cause us to repent, ver. 20.

59:4.text: Justice. They arraign unjustly. None call upon the just God, but trust in idols.
Iniquity. They kill themselves, while they strive to injure others, Psalm 7:15., and Micheas 2:1.

59:5.text: The young ones "burst through the viper's sides."
So the works of the wicked are useless or destructive.

59:8.text: Peace, or prosperity. They quarrel with all, and ruin themselves, Psalm xiii.

59:9.text: Therefore. The wicked Jews nevertheless confess that their sins prove their destruction.

59:10.text: Dead. The Jews will not recognize Christ, notwithstanding the prophecies and miracles.

59:11.text: Judgment, that God would avenge us, (ver. 9.) and regard our fasts, Isaias 58:3.

59:14.text: In. Where truth is disregarded, there can be no justice.

59:16.text: Himself, to arrest his arm, stretched out to chastise his son; or to second him. There is nothing in man to stop God's vengeance. He therefore pardons out of his own goodness, Isaias 59:2., and 63:4.
He became man to redeem us, as no pure mortal could do it.

59:17.text: Justice. None can blame his conduct.

59:19.text: Cyrus, the figurative redeemer, proceeds rapidly.

59:20.text: To Sion. Septuagint, "from Sion, and will turn away iniquity from Jacob. (21.) And this," etc.
St. Paul hence proves that the Jews will at last be converted, Romans 11:26. The return of the captives prefigured this event.

59:21.text: Covenant. Note here a clear promise of perpetual orthodoxy to the Church of Christ.
She hath still the spirit of truth.
None will apply this to the synagogue, which is visibly in the dark, and abandoned.

60:1.text: O Jerusalem, is not in Hebrew or St. Jerome, but in the Septuagint. Some few things may refer to the terrestrial Jerusalem, though the prophet speaks chiefly of the celestial and of the Church.
Lord, very great. Christ came to save us.
God prevents by his grace, but man must co-operate to be justified.

60:2.text: People. Babylon shall suffer, while thou art relieved.
The Gentiles continue in darkness till they embrace the faith, ver. 3.
Only those who are in the Church receive the light of truth.

60:3.text: Rising. The three wise men were the first. [Matthew ii.]

60:4.text: Rise up. St. Jerome, "suck," as the Hebrew may imply.
Septuagint, "shall be carried on the shoulders."
This may refer to the captives and to the Church.

60:5.text: Wonder. Hebrew and Septuagint in St. Jerome, "fear." This sensation is often mixed with joy, Matthew 28:8.
Thee. No such nations joined the Jews, as they did the Church.

60:6.text: Epha. Abraham's grandson, who dwelt near his father, Madian, in Arabia, which was famous for camels.
Saba. India mittit ebur, molles sua thura Sabaei?
The Arabians embraced the gospel, but never brought their treasures to Jerusalem.
The three kings came on swift beasts to adore Christ, and fulfilled his prophecy, Matthew 2:

60:7.text: Cedar and Nabaioth sprung from Ismael, and dwelt in desert Arabia, under tents, feeding flocks.
They also were converted to Christ.

60:8.text: Clouds. They are thy children, accompanied by strangers.

60:9.text: Afar. All nations shall receive the gospel. Many made presents to the temple, after the return of the Jews.

60:9.src: Tertullian; Origen; Ven. Bede; St. Chrysostom, etc.; Worthington
60:9.text: The islands, Great Britain, etc., embrace the faith.

60:10.text: To thee. The Persian monarchs (Calmet) were mostly favourable to the Jews.
The Gentiles help to form the Church, which rejects no one, ver. 11.

60:12.text: Desolation. Though the Machabees conquered several nations, this can only be verified in the Church of Christ, to which God has subjected all; so that out of his faith none can be saved, Hebrews 2:8.

60:13.text: Glory; cedar, which was chiefly used in building the temple, 1 Esdras 3:7.
This must be explained of the saints, who founded the Church, etc.
Emperors became Christians, with the most potent nations.

60:14.text: Feet. Protestants, "shall bow themselves down at the soles of thy feet." This posture is not then essentially idolatrous.

Jerusalem shall be rebuilt by those who destroyed it, Isaias 49:17. It is not easy to prove this of the earthly city: but the pagans, who persecuted the Church, have embraced her communion, and begged to receive baptism.

60:16.text: Kings. Thou shalt be treated like royal babes, Isaias 49:23.
Septuagint, "and thou shalt eat the riches of kings."

60:17.text: Visitation. Septuagint, "give thy chiefs in peace, and thy bishops in justice." St. Clement of Rome (ad Corinthians) reads, "I will appoint their bishops in justice, and their deacons in faith."
The Scripture thus specifies the name and duties of the pastors of the Church

60:18.text: Gates. Jerusalem was not less corrupt after the captivity than before, if we except idolatry.
Heaven alone enjoys a perfect peace and freedom from sin, (St. Cyril, etc.) though the Church is always holy.

60:19.text: Thou shalt, etc. In this latter part of the chapter, the prophet passes from the illustrious promises made to the Church militant on earth, to the glory of the Church triumphant in heaven.
Glory. St. John seems to have copied this, Apocalypse xviii., etc.

60:22.text: The least of the apostles shall bring many converts, (Calmet) or shall be spiritual (Haydock) governor of a great city, (Micheas 5:2.) in the Church militant.
A small shoot, or family, in the Church, shall produce many others.

61:1.text: Lord. Hebrew adds, "God." Adonai seems to have been inserted to prevent the pronunciation of Jehovah, (Kennicott) which alone occurs in the Septuagint, Arabic, and in St. Luke, 4:18.

Thus Elohim may have been substituted for Jehovah, Genesis 22:8., as [in] [Genesis xxii.] ver. 14, "Abraham called the name of that place Jehovah jireh, because he had said that day on the mount: Jehovah will provide" a victim, even Jesus Christ in the same place. Perhaps no part of the Bible is "so absurdly translated" as this, (Kennicott) by Protestants.

St. Luke follows the Septuagint in his quotation, only instead of to preach a, etc., he has an explanation, to set at liberty them that are bruised. Isaias may here speak of himself, (Chaldean) yet only as a figure of Christ. The Jews admit that the Messias is meant. Christ had received the Holy Spirit at the Jordan, John 1:32. He performed these works, (Luke 7:22.) particularly addressing his discourse to the meek and poor, Sophonias 3:12., and Zacharias 11:7., and 1 Corinthians 1:26.
He was not anointed with oil, like Aaron, but with the Holy Ghost; so that of his fullness others must receive, Acts 10:39., etc.

61:2.text: Year of Jubilee, (Jeremias 25:11.) when the Jews should be delivered, as a figure of Christ's redemption.
Vengeance, when the Chaldeans, etc., should perish, (Calmet) and all obstinate sinners, at the day of judgment.

61:3.text: Glorify. The rulers shall act with justice, Isaias 60:17, 21.

61:4.text: Ruins, as the Jews did, Isaias 58:12. The apostles preached to the Gentiles, who had been long neglected.

61:5.text: Vines. Bishops were soon chosen from among the Gentiles. The Machabees subdued the neighbouring nations, 1 Machabees 15:28.

61:6.text: Priests. They were greatly honoured, (Exodus 19:6.) so that the sons of David had the appellation. The Jews had still to labour as before. Christians become heirs to these promises, and are styled a royal priesthood, 1 Peter 2:9., and Apocalypse 1:6. They have received the Scriptures from the Jews, and employ human sciences for the advancement of religion. The wisest pagans yield to the force of truth.

61:7.text: Part: God, or the land. Converts shall bless God for having withdrawn them from the crowd of infidels, and they will rejoice in suffering for his sake, (Calmet) preferring their lot before that of unbelievers. Before this change the apostles grieved.

61:8.text: Holocaust. The pagans saw such things were improper victims.
Therefore will I subject the strangers to you, ver. 5.
Truth. I will grant a sure reward.
Covenant. These of Nehemias and the Machabees were soon forgotten: but Christ's covenant shall abide for ever.

61:9.text: Blessed. The Jews are visibly the reverse. The Church flourishes in spite of domestic and foreign enemies.

61:10.text: Jewels. Apocalypse 21:2. Jerusalem, or rather the Church, praises God.

61:11.text: Nations, whose conversion is implicitly foretold. All behold the justice which God has treated both his people and their oppressors.

62:1.text: Rest, as long as God grants me life, or till I behold the Saviour; or Cyrus, the figure of Christ.
True preachers will not be silent on account of any threats, but will labour for the Church, 2 Timothy 2:

62:2.text: One. No profane historian mentions what Cyrus did for the Jews; but all the world knows how much Christ has favoured his Church.

62:2.src: St. Ignatius of Antioch, ad Magnesians; St. Jerome
62:2.text: Name. Thou shalt be no longer the rebellious Jerusalem, but the spouse and chosen people, the Church of Christ.

62:4.text: Forsaken. Yet the synagogue was again rejected, at the death of Christ. The promises naturally relate to his Church.
Inhabited. Protestants retain the Hebrew words Chephtsi-bah, "my delight in her," and Beulah, (marginal note) "married."

62:5.text: Dwell. Hebrew, "marry." They shall be attached to their country.
Thee. He shall love thee as a bridegroom does one whom he has lately married. Christ never abandons his virgin spouse.
Isaias speaks of some state of the Jews which has not yet taken place.

62:6.text: Watchmen; priests and prophets, (Matthew 2:1., and Psalm 133:2.; Calmet) or angels.
The synagogue has been long destitute of guides, but the Catholic Church has an uninterrupted succession of watchful pastors.
Mindful whose duty it is to remind him of his promises, and to recite the Church office.
In these faithful watchman the Church is always visible.

62:8.text: Hand; inviolably, Deuteronomy 32:40. The Persians deemed this oath most sacred.
Darius, just expiring, said to Polycrates, "By thee I give this right hand to Alexander," (Plut.[Plutarch?]) that he may revenge my death.
Wine. The Church cannot be deprived of her faith or of her God.

62:10.text: People, that they may return to Judea, and be converted to Christ.

62:11.text: Though Cyrus was a figure of Christ, he was as much beneath him as earth is below heaven, Zacharias 9:9.

63:1.text: Edom. Edom and Bosra (a strong city of Edom) are here taken in a mystical sense for the enemies of Christ and his Church.
St. Jerome with reason finds it difficult to explain it of Christ, as it regards the Machabees.

The first six verses are applied to our Saviour's ascension, which excites the admiration of angels, Psalm 23:7. Judas, the Machabee, the glorious figure of Christ, is introduced speaking in this and the following chapter.
